fable_text: In the house of a certain farmer there lived a snake who regularly came to the table and was fed on scraps of food. Not long afterwards the farmer grew rich, but then he became angry at the snake and tried to attack him with an axe. The farmer then lost his wealth and he realized that he had prospered because of the good luck he had gained from the snake before having wounded him. The farmer then begged the snake to forgive him for his evil deed, and the snake replied, 'You are sorry for what you have done, but you must not expect me to be your faithful friend until this scar heals. It is not possible for me to be truly reconciled to you until all thought of that treacherous axe has left my mind.'
ground_truth_moral: He who harms someone should henceforth be treated with suspicion.

summaries.direct_moral: Trust once broken can never be fully restored while the memory of the betrayal remains.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Trust once shattered by betrayal cannot be fully restored as long as the memory of the injury remains.
summaries.proverb: Injuries may be forgiven, but they are not forgotten.
summaries.cot_proverb: Injuries may be forgiven, but they are not forgotten.

fable_text: There was a snake who used to lurk around the front door of a farmer's house. One day the snake struck the man's son, biting him on the foot. The boy died on the spot. The boy's parents were filled with immense sorrow and the grief-stricken father seized his axe and tried to kill the malevolent snake. When the snake fled his pursuer, the man hurried after him, raising his weapon, determined to strike, but as the farmer was about to deal the snake a deadly blow, he missed and managed only to cut off the tip of his tail. The man was terrified at the thought that he might have killed the snake, so he took cakes and water along with honey and salt and called to the snake, wanting to make peace with him. The snake, however, only hissed softly at the farmer from where he had hidden himself in the rocks and said: 'Man, do not trouble yourself any longer: there can be no possible friendship between us any more. When I look upon my tail, I am in pain. The same is true for you: whenever you look again upon the grave of your son, you will not be able to live in peace with me.'
ground_truth_moral: Injuries may be forgiven, but not forgotten.

summaries.direct_moral: Mutual injuries create an insurmountable barrier to true reconciliation.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Lasting injuries and their permanent reminders make genuine reconciliation impossible.
summaries.proverb: Deep injuries leave lasting scars.
summaries.cot_proverb: Injuries are easier to forgive than to forget.

fable_text: Two wolves crossed paths one day and began to talk about the fact that they were an object of universal hatred. People would even start yelling at them when they had no intention of doing anything wrong. One of the wolves then said to the other, 'It's all because they've never seen us do anything good! If the people saw us do them a favour even just once, then they would be more inclined to expect us to be good later on.' The second wolf asked, 'What good thing do you think we can do that will convince the people of our good behaviour?' The first wolf replied, 'Let's leave the woods and go into the fields where we can help the people gathering sheaves!' So the wolves came out of the woods and went into the fields and gathered the sheaves, just as they had planned. But as soon as the people saw the wolves in the field, they immediately chased them away, shouting and screaming. The wolves were surprised and said, 'What do they mean by shouting at us like that, when we are not doing any harm and only trying to help them?' The other wolf replied, 'Let's go back home and act as we did in the past. Whether we help the people or harass them, they are going to hate us just the same!'
ground_truth_moral: When wicked people do not get the reward and thanks they expect, they immediately put a stop to their good works.

summaries.direct_moral: A bad reputation is difficult to overcome, regardless of one's intentions.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: An established reputation is often more powerful than the reality of one's actions.
summaries.proverb: Give a dog a bad name and hang him.
summaries.cot_proverb: A bad reputation is easier to earn than to lose.

fable_text: A rich old farmer, who felt that he had not many more days to live, called his sons to his bedside. 'My sons,' he said, 'heed what I have to say to you. Do not on any account part with the estate that has belonged to our family for so many generations. Somewhere on it is hidden a rich treasure. I do not know the exact spot, but it is there, and you will surely find it. Spare no energy and leave no spot unturned in your search.' The father died, and no sooner was he in his grave than the sons set to work digging with all their might, turning up every foot of ground with their spades, and going over the whole farm two or three times. No hidden gold did they find; but at harvest time when they had settled their accounts and had pocketed a rich profit far greater than that of any of their neighbors, they understood that the treasure their father had told them about was the wealth of a bountiful crop.
ground_truth_moral: Man's greatest treasure consists in work.

summaries.direct_moral: Industry is itself a treasure.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Industry is itself a treasure.
summaries.proverb: Industry is itself a treasure.
summaries.cot_proverb: Industry is itself a treasure.

fable_text: There was a timid old man who had an only child, a son, who was generally high-spirited and who wanted to go hunting. In a dream the father saw his son lying dead, killed by a lion. Afraid that this might really happen and that the dream might actually come true, he built a house for the men of the family, and it was an extremely beautiful house, with high ceilings and sturdy walls and full of sunlight. He then enclosed his son inside this house, locked away under guard. To keep his son from becoming sad, he had the walls decorated with pictures of various animals, and among all these animals there was a painted lion. Staring at the lion, the boy felt even more sad, and he eventually approached the lion and said, 'O you wicked animal, because you showed that lying dream to my father's eyes you are able to keep me prisoner here, watched by guards as if I were a woman. But why do I attack you only with words, and not with an act of violence as well?' The boy then dashed his hands against the lion, intending to scratch its eyes out, but instead a sliver of wood came off and stabbed him under his fingernail. This soon brought about a burning inflammation of the flesh, and although the desperate father did everything he could, it was all to no avail. The infection spread until it reached the boy's groin, and thus brought his life to an end. The old man was unable to save his child, who had been destined to die because of a lion who was not even alive.
ground_truth_moral: We had better bear our troubles bravely than try to escape them.

summaries.direct_moral: One cannot escape the fate they seek to avoid.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: One cannot escape his destiny by taking measures to avoid it.
summaries.proverb: No man can escape his fate.
summaries.cot_proverb: There is no escaping one's destiny.

fable_text: A fisherman was pulling in the net which he had just cast and, as luck would have it, the net was filled with all kinds of sea creatures. The little fish escaped to the bottom of the net and slipped out through its many holes, but the big fish was caught and lay stretched out flat aboard the boat.
ground_truth_moral: To be small is a way to stay safe and avoid problems, whereas you rarely see a man with a big reputation who is able to keep out of danger.

summaries.direct_moral: Obscurity often provides safety where prominence invites peril.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Greatness often brings peril, while obscurity provides safety.
summaries.proverb: Obscurity is a safeguard.
summaries.cot_proverb: Obscurity is often a safeguard.

fable_text: A fisherman was catching fish by the sea. A monkey saw him, and wanted to imitate what he was doing. The man went away into a little cave to take a rest, leaving his net on the beach. The monkey came and grabbed the net, thinking that he too would go fishing. But since he didn't know anything about it and had not had any training, the monkey got tangled up in the net, fell into the sea, and was drowned. The fisherman seized the monkey when he was already done for and said, 'You wretched creature! Your lack of judgment and stupid behaviour has cost you your life!'
ground_truth_moral: Imitation is no substitute for knowledge.

summaries.direct_moral: Blindly imitating others without the necessary skills or knowledge leads to disaster.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Blind imitation without the necessary skills or training often leads to disaster.
summaries.proverb: Never attempt what you do not understand.
summaries.cot_proverb: Attempting what you do not understand leads to ruin.

fable_text: Some fishermen were hauling in their net. It was quite heavy, so the fishermen made merry and danced for joy, thinking that they had landed a very big catch. Yet when they finally dragged it in, they found that the net contained only a few fish, together with a very large stone. The fishermen now grew extremely despondent, not so much because of the lack of fish but because they had been expecting just the opposite. Then one of the fishermen, an experienced old man, remarked, 'Let's not take it too hard, my friends! Given that grief seems to be the sister of joy, then we must expect to put up with some suffering precisely because we were so elated at first.'
ground_truth_moral: We have to endure reversals of fortune, since we know that life is a matter of luck.

summaries.direct_moral: Sorrow is the inevitable companion of joy.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Extreme disappointment is the natural consequence of premature or excessive expectation.
summaries.proverb: Sorrow treads on the heels of joy.
summaries.cot_proverb: Expectation is the mother of disappointment.

fable_text: Some fishermen had gone out fishing, and when they had struggled for a long time but had not managed to catch anything, they became very downcast and prepared to turn back. All of a sudden a tuna fish who was being chased by some bigger fish leaped into their boat. The men seized the tuna fish and went home rejoicing.
ground_truth_moral: Luck often bestows the things that skill cannot obtain.

summaries.direct_moral: Fortune often provides what labor cannot.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Fortune often provides what effort cannot achieve.
summaries.proverb: Fortune often provides what labor cannot.
summaries.cot_proverb: Chance often gives what effort cannot.

fable_text: A fisherman was fishing in a river. He stretched out his nets and covered the river's stream from one side to the other. He then tied a stone to a piece of rope and struck the water with it so that the fish would flee and fall unwittingly into the net. Someone who lived in that neighbourhood saw what the man was doing and began to complain, because by agitating the water in this way he deprived them of clear water to drink. The fisherman answered, 'But if I do not disturb the river, I will have no choice but to die of hunger!'
ground_truth_moral: Some never understand the actions of others.

summaries.direct_moral: Agitators find their greatest profit in troubled waters.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Some people thrive only by creating turmoil and confusion for others.
summaries.proverb: Fish in troubled waters.
summaries.cot_proverb: It is best fishing in troubled waters.

fable_text: A flea once asked an ox, 'Given that you are so exceedingly large and strong, why do you suffer this enslavement to people day after day? Look at me: I lacerate their flesh without pity, drinking great gulps of their blood!' The ox said, 'I cannot help but be grateful to the human race, since I am cherished and loved by them to an extraordinary degree, and since they often rub my forehead and shoulders.' The flea admitted, 'Woe is me! This rubbing of which you are so fond is the worst thing that can happen to me: when they do that, I die.'
ground_truth_moral: A reward for one might be destruction for another.

summaries.direct_moral: What is a blessing to one may be a curse to another.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: What one finds a source of comfort, another finds a source of destruction.
summaries.proverb: One man’s meat is another man’s poison.
summaries.cot_proverb: One man’s blessing is another’s bane.

fable_text: A man finally caught a flea that had been bothering him terribly. He shouted at the flea, 'Just who do you think you are, feeding on all the limbs of my body here and there, eating me up as you please?' The flea responded, 'That is how we live! Please don't kill me; I cannot be causing you too much harm.' The man laughed at the flea and said, 'I'm going to kill you here and now with my very own hands: any kind of evil, whether it is big or small, should not be allowed to exist under any circumstances whatsoever!'
ground_truth_moral: No mercy should be shown to someone who is wicked, regardless of whether his wickedness is great or small.

summaries.direct_moral: No evil should be tolerated, regardless of its size.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: No evil should be tolerated, regardless of its magnitude.
summaries.proverb: No evil is small enough to be tolerated.
summaries.cot_proverb: Nip evil in the bud, however small it may be.

fable_text: When the honey had been spilled in the pantry, some flies flew up and landed on it. Their feet got stuck and they could not fly away. As they were drowning in the honey, the flies remarked, 'Oh, woe is us! For the sake of a brief banquet, we are going to die!'
ground_truth_moral: Pleasure bought with pains, hurts.

summaries.direct_moral: Fleeting pleasures often lead to fatal consequences.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Pursuing momentary gratification often leads to ultimate ruin.
summaries.proverb: Greed leads to ruin.
summaries.cot_proverb: Pleasure bought with life is too dearly bought.

fable_text: A fly alighted on a wagon pole and began to harass the mule. 'You are moving so slowly!' she said, 'Can't you walk any faster than this? Watch out, or I will pierce your neck with my sting!' The mule replied, 'I don't care what you say: the only thing that scares me is that man who sits up there in front regulating my pace with his pliant whip and keeping my head in check with the foaming bit. I've had enough of you and your silly boasting: I know full well when I am supposed go slowly and when I am supposed to run!'
ground_truth_moral: Ignore complainers who have no influence.

summaries.direct_moral: Empty threats from the powerless carry no weight.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The empty threats of the powerless are of no consequence to those who answer to a real authority.
summaries.proverb: The powerless boast in vain.
summaries.cot_proverb: Empty threats from the powerless are of no account.

fable_text: A man was riding a pregnant mare and she gave birth to her foal while they were still on the road. The new-born foal followed directly behind his mother but soon became unsteady on his feet. The foal then said to the man, 'Look, you can see that I am very small and not strong enough to travel. If you leave me here, I am sure to die. But if you carry me away from here back to your home and bring me up, then later on, when I am grown, I shall let you ride me.'
ground_truth_moral: We should do favours for someone who can do us a good deed in return.

summaries.direct_moral: An act of kindness toward the weak is an investment in future rewards.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Investment in the potential of the vulnerable yields future rewards.
summaries.proverb: Kindness shown today is an investment for tomorrow.
summaries.cot_proverb: A kindness shown today is an investment for tomorrow.

fable_text: Thanks to his excellent qualities, Hercules was received into heaven. He saluted the gods who came to congratulate him one after another, but when he was approached by Plutus, the god of wealth and the son of Fortune, Hercules turned his eyes aside. Father Jupiter asked him why he did this. Hercules answered, 'I hate the god of riches: he is a friend to the wicked who corrupts the entire world by throwing his money around!'
ground_truth_moral: Coffers of cash put a stop to honest traffic in praise.

summaries.direct_moral: Wealth is often the ally of the wicked and a source of universal corruption.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Wealth is often a corrupting force that rewards the wicked and remains incompatible with true virtue.
summaries.proverb: Wealth is the friend of the wicked.
summaries.cot_proverb: Wealth is often the companion of the wicked.

fable_text: A horse was suffering from old age, so instead of serving in battle he was sent to the mill to turn the stones. Condemned to this daily grind and denied the glory of battle, the horse wept over his present way of life, remembering his past career. 'Woe is me!' the horse exclaimed. 'Listen, miller: when I was destined for the battlefield, I was decorated all over with armour and I had a man to serve me as my groom. As things are now, I don't know how it happened that I now have this mill to handle instead of a battle.' The miller said to the horse, 'Can't you keep quiet? I've had quite enough of your raving about the past: Luck can change people's lives for better or for worse!'
ground_truth_moral: Accept the changes in life.

summaries.direct_moral: Fortune is fickle and subject to change.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Fortune is fickle, and no person is guaranteed that their past success will protect them from future hardship.
summaries.proverb: Fortune is fickle.
summaries.cot_proverb: Fortune is fickle.

fable_text: A fox happened to find a mask used for performing tragedies and, after turning it this way and that several times, she remarked, 'So full of beauty, so lacking in brains!'
ground_truth_moral: Outside show is a poor substitute for inner worth.

summaries.direct_moral: Physical beauty is no substitute for intelligence.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Outward appearance is no substitute for internal substance.
summaries.proverb: A fair face may hide an empty mind.
summaries.cot_proverb: A fine appearance is no substitute for inward worth.

fable_text: The hare laughed at the tortoise's feet but the tortoise declared, 'I will beat you in a race!' The hare replied, 'Those are just words. Race with me, and you'll see! Who will mark out the track and serve as our umpire?' 'The fox,' replied the tortoise, 'since she is honest and highly intelligent.' When the time for the race had been decided upon, the tortoise did not delay, but immediately took off down the race course. The hare, however, lay down to take a nap, confident in the speed of his feet. Then, when the hare eventually made his way to the finish line, he found that the tortoise had already won.
ground_truth_moral: Slow and steady wins the race.

summaries.direct_moral: Slow and steady wins the race.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Persistence and consistency often prevail over superior ability hindered by overconfidence.
summaries.proverb: Slow and steady wins the race.
summaries.cot_proverb: Slow and steady wins the race.

fable_text: There was a grapevine next to a river, and a thorn bush had been planted as a fence around the vine. When the river rose, the thorn bush was swept away by the water, and a snake who had entwined himself in the thorns was also carried away. When someone saw the snake riding on the thorn bush, he said, 'A wicked ship, and worthy of its sailor!'
ground_truth_moral: A wicked person deservedly comes to a bad end by keeping company with other wicked people.

summaries.direct_moral: The wicked are fitly matched with their misfortunes.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The wicked are appropriately matched by their misfortunes.
summaries.proverb: Like ship, like sailor.
summaries.cot_proverb: Like to like.

fable_text: Driven by hunger, a fox tried to reach some grapes hanging high on the vine. Although she leaped with all her strength, she couldn't manage to reach the grapes. As she went away, the fox remarked, 'Oh, you aren't even ripe yet! I don't need any sour grapes.'
ground_truth_moral: It's easy to despise what you cannot have.

summaries.direct_moral: It is easy to despise what you cannot attain.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: It is easy to despise what you cannot attain.
summaries.proverb: It is easy to despise what you cannot get.
summaries.cot_proverb: It is easy to despise what you cannot get.

fable_text: The hare said to the fox, 'They say you are very artful, fox. What art is it that you practice exactly?' The fox replied, 'If you don't know my arts, I will have you to dinner so that you can get a taste of my art.' The hare followed the fox to her den but the fox had nothing there to eat except for the hare himself. The hare exclaimed, 'I have learned to my cost that your name does not derive from any kind of artistry but from fraud!'
ground_truth_moral: Overly curious people often pay a very high price for recklessly indulging their curiosity.

summaries.direct_moral: Never trust the hospitality of a known deceiver.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Curiosity about the methods of the treacherous often results in becoming their victim.
summaries.proverb: Never trust the hospitality of a known deceiver.
summaries.cot_proverb: Cunning in the wicked is but another name for fraud.

fable_text: A thirsty hare had gone done into a well to drink the water. He took a good long drink, and when he wanted to get back out again, he found himself trapped with no means of escape. It was a very discouraging situation. A fox then arrived on the scene and when she found the hare she said to him, 'You have made a very serious mistake indeed: you should have first decided on a way to get out and only then gone down into the well!'
ground_truth_moral: People who act impulsively can end up in regrettable situations.

summaries.direct_moral: Look before you leap.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Consider the end before the beginning.
summaries.proverb: Look before you leap.
summaries.cot_proverb: Look before you leap.

fable_text: They say that hyenas change their nature every year, so that sometimes they are male and sometimes female. So when a hyena saw a fox and criticized her for having spurned her friendly overtures, the fox replied, 'Don't blame me! Blame your own nature, which makes it impossible for me to tell whether you would be my girlfriend or my boyfriend!'
ground_truth_moral: Ambiguity in character can lead to mistrust.

summaries.direct_moral: Inconsistency of character makes trust impossible.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Inconsistency of character precludes the formation of stable and trusting relationships.
summaries.proverb: Consistency is the foundation of trust.
summaries.cot_proverb: Trust cannot be placed in the inconsistent.

fable_text: The story goes that a sow who had delivered a whole litter of piglets loudly accosted a lioness, 'How many children do you breed?' asked the sow. 'I breed only one,' said the lioness, 'but he is very well bred!'
ground_truth_moral: Quality is more valuable than quantity.

summaries.direct_moral: Quality is more important than quantity.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Quality is more important than quantity.
summaries.proverb: Quality over quantity.
summaries.cot_proverb: Quality is better than quantity.

fable_text: A fox saw a lion that had been imprisoned in a cage. The fox approached the lion and insulted him very rudely. The lion then said to her, 'You are not responsible for my disgrace; the cause is my unfortunate situation.'
ground_truth_moral: Chance sometimes rules.

summaries.direct_moral: Misfortune emboldens the weak to insult the strong.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Cowards take advantage of the misfortunes of others to insult those they would otherwise fear.
summaries.proverb: Misfortune emboldens the cowardly.
summaries.cot_proverb: It is easy to be brave when your enemy is bound.

fable_text: A lion had grown old and weak. He pretended to be sick, which was just a ruse to make the other animals come pay their respects so that he could eat them all up, one by one. The fox also came to see the lion, but she greeted him from outside the cave. The lion asked the fox why she didn't come in. The fox replied, 'Because I see the tracks of those going in, but none coming out.'
ground_truth_moral: Take warning from the misfortunes of others.

summaries.direct_moral: Learn from the misfortunes of others.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Wise individuals learn from the misfortunes of others to avoid similar dangers.
summaries.proverb: Learn from the misfortunes of others.
summaries.cot_proverb: He is wise who learns from the misfortunes of others.

fable_text: A fox and a monkey were travelling along the same road. They passed through a cemetery, and the monkey said to the fox, 'All these dead people were the freedmen of my ancestors.' The fox then said to the monkey, 'This is an opportune moment for you to tell such lies: not a single one of the people entombed in this place can rise up and refute what you say!'
ground_truth_moral: Deceivers often exploit the inability of others to challenge their falsehoods.

summaries.direct_moral: Liars boast most boldly when they know their claims cannot be disproven.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: People boast most when they are certain they cannot be refuted.
summaries.proverb: Liars boast loudest when none can refute them.
summaries.cot_proverb: A liar is boldest when there are no witnesses to contradict him.

fable_text: A fox was out walking one night next to a river. She saw the light of the moon reflected in the water and mistook it for a piece of cheese. The fox started to lap at the water, thinking that if she could drink up all the water, she would find the cheese in the dry riverbed. The fox lapped and lapped at the water until finally she choked and died.
ground_truth_moral: Greedy men chase after profit with such an intensity that they destroy themselves without achieving anything.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed for illusions leads to self-destruction.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The blind pursuit of an illusion leads to self-destruction.
summaries.proverb: Greed leads to ruin.
summaries.cot_proverb: Greed often leads to its own destruction.

fable_text: A partridge had seated herself high on a perch when a fox came up to her and said, 'How beautiful you are to look at: your legs are so red! your mouth is like coral! Ah, if only you were sleeping, you would be even more lovely...' The partridge believed the fox and closed her eyes, and the fox immediately grabbed her. In a voice choked with sobs, the partridge said to the fox, 'I beg you, in the name of all your artful wiles, please say my name before you eat me up.' As the fox's mouth opened to pronounce the word 'partridge,' the partridge flew out and escaped. The fox said sadly, 'Woe is me, what need was there for me to speak?' The partridge likewise said, 'Woe is me, what reason was there for me to close my eyes, when I wasn't even sleepy?'
ground_truth_moral: Stay vigilant and think before you speak.

summaries.direct_moral: Remain vigilant against flattery and keep your mouth shut when silence serves you best.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Vigilance is the best defense against flattery, while silence is the surest safeguard of success.
summaries.proverb: Keep your eyes open and your mouth shut.
summaries.cot_proverb: Keep your eyes open and your mouth shut.

fable_text: A wolf had assembled an immense store of booty in his den so that he could feast on all sorts of delicacies for months into the future. When the fox found out about this she paid a visit to the wolf and spoke to him in anxious tones, 'Why should I be deprived of your company these many days? I've been in tears since you have not come outside.' The wolf replied spitefully, 'You didn't come here out of any concern for me; you're just hoping to get something. I know you must have had some reason for coming here: you are trying to trick me!' This made the fox extremely angry, so she went to the shepherd and said to him, 'Would you like it if I turned the enemy of your flock over to you today so that you wouldn't have to worry about him anymore? The shepherd replied, 'I will be at your service and will give you whatever you want.' The fox immediately led the shepherd to where the wolf was hiding and the shepherd killed the wolf with his lance. He then let the spiteful fox eat her fill of someone else's larder. Eventually the fox fell foul of hunters and was caught by their hounds. As she was being torn to pieces, the fox exclaimed, 'Just as I committed a serious crime, now I am going to die, since I brought about someone else's death.'
ground_truth_moral: Take pleasure when thieves destroy themselves.

summaries.direct_moral: Those who plot the destruction of others will eventually meet their own downfall.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Treachery towards others inevitably leads to one’s own destruction.
summaries.proverb: Treachery returns to haunt its author.
summaries.cot_proverb: He who digs a pit for another falls into it himself.

fable_text: A fox got caught in a trap and part of her tail was cut off as she escaped. She was so ashamed that she didn't think life was worth living. Then she realized that if she could persuade the other foxes to do the same thing her own shame would be hidden, as it would be shared by all the foxes. Accordingly, she summoned the other foxes and asked them to cut off their tails, maintaining that this was the one part of their body that was unseemly, nothing but an extra appendage which they had to carry around. One of the other foxes scoffed in reply, 'If it weren't for the fact that it is in your interest to do this thing, you never would have proposed it!'
ground_truth_moral: Distrust advice from someone who stands to gain.

summaries.direct_moral: Distrust advice that is motivated solely by the advisor's own misfortune or self-interest.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Distrust advice that is motivated by the speaker's desire to normalize their own misfortunes.
summaries.proverb: Distrust interested advice.
summaries.cot_proverb: Distrust advice that is given for the speaker's own sake.

fable_text: The fox had never seen a lion before, so when she happened to meet the lion for the first time she all but died of fright. The second time she saw him, she was still afraid, but not as much as before. The third time, the fox was bold enough to go right up to the lion and speak to him.
ground_truth_moral: Familiarity makes it easy to confront even frightening situations.

summaries.direct_moral: Familiarity reduces fear.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Familiarity tames fear.
summaries.proverb: Familiarity breeds contempt.
summaries.cot_proverb: Familiarity breeds contempt.

fable_text: The lion had grown old and sick and was lying in his cave. All the animals, except for the fox, had come to visit their king. The wolf seized this opportunity to denounce the fox in front of the lion, complaining that the fox showed no respect for the lion, who was the common master of them all. Indeed, the fox had not even come to pay the ailing lion a visit! The fox arrived just in time to hear the end of the wolf's speech. The lion roared at the fox, but the fox asked for a chance to explain herself. 'After all,' said the fox, 'which one of all the animals assembled here has helped you as I have, travelling all over the world in order to seek out and discover from the doctors a remedy for your illness?' The lion ordered the fox to describe the remedy immediately, and the fox replied, 'You must flay a living wolf and wrap yourself in his skin while it is still warm.' When the wolf had been killed, the fox laughed and said, 'It is better to put your master in a good mood, not a bad one.'
ground_truth_moral: Someone who plots against others falls into his own trap.

summaries.direct_moral: He who plots against others often falls victim to his own scheme.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: He who plots the destruction of others often brings about his own ruin.
summaries.proverb: He who digs a pit for another falls into it himself.
summaries.cot_proverb: He who sows snares for others falls into them himself.

fable_text: Tiberius Caesar was on his way to Naples and had arrived at his estate in Misenum which had been built by Lucullus on a high hill overlooking the Sicilian sea on one side and the Tuscan sea on the other. When Caesar was walking about in the cheerful greenery, one of his household stewards turned up, dressed in a fancy fringed tunic of Egyptian cotton hanging down from his shoulders. The man began to sprinkle the sizzling hot ground with water from a wooden basin, making a great show of his diligence as Caesar's attendant, but everyone just laughed at him. The man then ran ahead to the next walkway, using some shortcuts known only to himself, and he started settling the dust in that spot as well. When Caesar recognized the man and realized what he was doing, he said, 'Hey you!' The man scampered up to Caesar, excited at the joyful prospect of what seemed a sure reward. Then Caesar's majestic person made the following joke: 'You have not accomplished much and your efforts have come to naught; if you want me to give you the slap that makes you a freedman, it will cost you much more than that!'
ground_truth_moral: Beware of those who appear busy but achieve little of value.

summaries.direct_moral: Empty showmanship and superficial effort do not merit great rewards.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Superficial flattery and performative diligence cannot substitute for genuine merit.
summaries.proverb: Empty industry earns no reward.
summaries.cot_proverb: Busy idleness earns no reward.

fable_text: The Lion hearing an odd kind of a hollow voice, and seeing nobody, started up. He listened again; the voice continued, and he shook with fear. At last seeing a Frog crawl out of the lake, and finding that the noise proceeded from that little creature, he spurned it to pieces with his feet.
ground_truth_moral: Imaginary fears are the worst.

summaries.direct_moral: Empty noise should not be mistaken for a real threat.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Fear of the unknown is often far greater than the reality of the threat.
summaries.proverb: Empty vessels make the most noise.
summaries.cot_proverb: Fear is often greater than the danger.

fable_text: There were two frogs who lived near one another. One lived in a deep pond that was far away from the road, but the other lived near the road where there was only a small amount of water. The frog who lived in the deep pond advised the other frog to move in with him so that they could share the pond between them and live a life that was less fraught with danger. The roadside frog refused the offer, saying that he had become accustomed to his home and couldn't tear himself away. Not long afterwards, he was crushed under the wheel of a passing wagon.
ground_truth_moral: People who stubbornly cling to unwise choices lose everything before they know it.

summaries.direct_moral: Stubborn attachment to dangerous habits leads to ruin.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Stubborn attachment to familiar habits, even when dangerous, leads to avoidable ruin.
summaries.proverb: Stubbornness leads to ruin.
summaries.cot_proverb: Habit is no shield against disaster.

fable_text: A mouse asked a frog to help her get across the river. The frog tied the mouse's front leg to her own back leg using a piece of string and they swam out to the middle of the stream. The frog then turned traitor and plunged down into the water, dragging the mouse along with her. The mouse's dead body floated up to the surface and was drifting along when a kite flew by and noticed something he could snatch. When he grabbed the mouse he also carried off her friend the frog. Thus the treacherous frog who had betrayed the mouse's life was likewise killed and eaten.
ground_truth_moral: People who do harm to others destroy themselves in the bargain.

summaries.direct_moral: Treachery often recoils upon the traitor.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Treachery eventually recoils upon the traitor.
summaries.proverb: Treachery recoils on the traitor.
summaries.cot_proverb: He who digs a pit for others falls into it himself.

fable_text: A frog looked out from her pond and saw a battle taking place between the bulls. 'Oh no!' she said, 'There is terrible danger in store for us.' Another frog asked her why she said this, since the bulls were fighting for control of the herd in their home far away from the frogs. The first frog explained, 'While their habitat may be separate from ours and our species not the same, the bull who is driven from the lordship of the meadow will come to find a secret hiding place here in the marsh, crushing us beneath his heavy hooves. That is why their frenzy is a matter of life and death for us!'
ground_truth_moral: Poor folk suffer when the high and mighty are at war with one another.

summaries.direct_moral: When the powerful fight, the humble suffer.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The quarrels of the powerful often result in the suffering of the weak.
summaries.proverb: When the great quarrel, the small suffer.
summaries.cot_proverb: When the powerful clash, the weak suffer the consequences.

fable_text: There was a viper who used to go to a certain spring in order to drink but he was driven away by a water-snake who was furious that the viper would not just stay on his own turf instead of encroaching on the water-snake's territory. Their dispute grew more and more fierce and finally the two of them decided to fight it out, agreeing that both the water and the land would be awarded to the winner. When the day for the fight had been decided, the frogs came to the viper and, since they hated the water-snake, they offered to be his allies and come to his aid during the battle. But when the battle began and the viper grappled with the water-snake, the frogs just sat there croaking, since they were not able to do anything else. In the end, the viper was victorious but he was furious with the frogs since they had failed to come to his aid as they had promised and, what was worse, they had sat there singing songs while he was doing battle. The frogs then said to the viper, 'But you should have known that we had nothing to offer you except the sound of our voices!'
ground_truth_moral: When you need someone to lend a hand, mere words are no help at all.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not rely on the promises of those who lack the power to act.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Judge the value of an ally by their capacity to act rather than the fervor of their promises.
summaries.proverb: Deeds, not words, are the true test of friendship.
summaries.cot_proverb: Deeds, not words, are the true proof of friendship.

fable_text: There were two frogs whose pond had dried up, so they went looking for a new place to live. When they came to a well, one of them thought that they should jump in immediately, but the other one said, 'Wait: what if the water were to dry up here too; how would we be able to get back out again?'
ground_truth_moral: Look before you leap.

summaries.direct_moral: Look before you leap.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Look before you leap.
summaries.proverb: Look before you leap.
summaries.cot_proverb: Look before you leap.

fable_text: When Zeus had fashioned man and woman, he ordered Hermes to take them to Gaia, the Earth, and to show them how to obtain food by digging in the ground. At first, the Earth refused to cooperate in Hermes' mission. Hermes then compelled her, saying that Zeus had ordered her to do so. Earth replied, 'Then let them dig as much as they like, but they will pay for it with groans and tears!'
ground_truth_moral: Easy gains often come with hidden costs and hardships.

summaries.direct_moral: Human survival and sustenance must be earned through hard labor and suffering.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Sustenance and survival are inextricably linked to labor and suffering.
summaries.proverb: Nothing is achieved without labor and sorrow.
summaries.cot_proverb: Every gain has its price in pain.

fable_text: The vine was covered with clusters of grapes and its shoots were as vigorous as its fruit. A goat began to eat the vine, indulging his outrageous appetite. He had already wreaked havoc on the fresh shoots when the vine said to him, 'You will pay for this outrage! The time will soon come when you will be sent as a holy victim to be sacrificed, and I myself will be the one to supply the wine for the libation!'
ground_truth_moral: The things that a person does to others will happen to him in turn.

summaries.direct_moral: Retribution often comes from the very source one has mistreated.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Those who commit injuries will eventually face retribution through the very objects of their malice.
summaries.proverb: Evil deeds return to their author.
summaries.cot_proverb: Evil-doers often provide the means for their own punishment.

fable_text: When the she-goats had persuaded Jupiter to give them beards, the male goats were extremely upset and began to complain that the women now had the same degree of dignity that they did. 'Come now,' said Jupiter, 'let the women enjoy this vain source of pride and stake their claim to the badges of your office so long as they cannot rival you in strength!'
ground_truth_moral: Bear that those who are inferior to you in merit should be like you in outside appearances.

summaries.direct_moral: True superiority lies in actual strength rather than superficial symbols of status.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True superiority resides in actual strength and character rather than in the superficial symbols of status.
summaries.proverb: The habit does not make the monk.
summaries.cot_proverb: Substance is more important than appearance.

fable_text: Some swans and some geese became friends and went out onto the meadows. As they were feeding contentedly together, they were discovered by hunters. The swans, owing to the swiftness of their bodies, were immediately able to take wing and fly away. The geese, however, were naturally more heavy and so they were left behind and caught by the hunters.
ground_truth_moral: Beware of enterprizes where the risks are not equal.

summaries.direct_moral: Natural advantages often determine survival in times of peril.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Sharing a common danger does not ensure a common escape for those with unequal natural advantages.
summaries.proverb: Associate with your equals.
summaries.cot_proverb: In a sudden danger, the slow pay the price.

fable_text: A stork had gone to her usual pond and saw a goose there who kept plunging down deep into the water. The stork then asked the goose what she was doing and the goose replied, 'We geese go down into the muddy bottom of the pond to look for food and to escape the hawk's attack.' The stork said, 'I am stronger than the hawk! You should be friends with me, and I will allow you to scoff at that other bird!' The goose agreed, and not long afterwards she called on the stork to come help her. The goose had not plunged into the water, so the hawk immediately swooped down and caught her up in his talons. As the hawk was about to devour her, the goose replied, 'A wretched death awaits anyone who puts his trust in such a worthless defender!'
ground_truth_moral: Do not expect to be defended by someone who cannot offer any protection.

summaries.direct_moral: Never abandon proven safety for the empty promises of a boastful protector.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: It is better to rely on proven methods of self-preservation than to trust the empty promises of a boastful protector.
summaries.proverb: Trust not the promises of a boaster.
summaries.cot_proverb: Better to rely on your own known defenses than on the empty promises of a braggart.

fable_text: The halcyon is a bird who is fond of deserted places and who always lives on the sea. They say that she makes her nest on the rocky cliffs of the coast in order to protect herself from human hunters. So when a certain halcyon was about to lay her eggs, she went to a promontory and found a rock jutting out towards the sea and decided to make her nest there. But when she went to look for food, it happened that the sea swelled under the blustering wind and reached as high as the halcyon's home and flooded the nest, killing her chicks. When the halcyon returned and saw what had happened, she said, 'What a fool I was to have protected myself against a plot hatched on the land by taking refuge here on the sea, when it is the sea that has utterly betrayed me!'
ground_truth_moral: Sometimes, the greatest threats come from those you trust.

summaries.direct_moral: In trying to avoid one danger, we often fall into a greater one.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: In fleeing one danger, we often fall into a far greater one.
summaries.proverb: Out of the frying pan, into the fire.
summaries.cot_proverb: In avoiding one evil, we often fall into a worse.

fable_text: There was a hunter who had caught a hare and was carrying it home. As he went along his way, he met a man on horseback who asked him for the hare, pretending that he wanted to buy it. As soon as he got the hare from the hunter, the horseman immediately took off at a gallop. The hunter began to pursue the horseman thinking that he might catch up with him. When the horseman finally disappeared into the distance, the hunter reluctantly said, 'Go ahead then! That hare is my gift to you.'
ground_truth_moral: A fool will make excuses for foolish behavior.

summaries.direct_moral: Make a virtue of necessity.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Make a virtue of necessity.
summaries.proverb: Make a virtue of necessity.
summaries.cot_proverb: Make a virtue of necessity.

fable_text: A certain hare happened to see a stag adorned with great branching horns. The hare complained to Jupiter that the hares were weak, defective creatures who were not feared by any of the other animals. Therefore, he wanted Jupiter to give him horns like those of the stag both for his own protection and to enhance his appearance. Jupiter told the hare that he would not be able to carry the heavy weight of the horns, but the hare replied that he was prepared to carry such horns with pride. Jupiter therefore commanded that the hare's head should be crowned with a pair of big, branching horns, but the hare was so weighed down by his burden that he wasn't able to run. As a result, the shepherds captured and killed him.
ground_truth_moral: It is often the case that people are greedy for things which they think will bring them honour, but which only result in their death and destruction.

summaries.direct_moral: Seeking strengths unsuited to your nature can lead to your ruin.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Acquiring attributes ill-suited to one’s nature can transform a perceived advantage into a fatal burden.
summaries.proverb: Do not desire what you cannot bear.
summaries.cot_proverb: A gift that does not suit the receiver is a burden, not a blessing.

fable_text: There was a rooster who was always quarrelling with another rooster, so he asked the hawk to judge their dispute. The rooster was actually hoping that when he brought the other rooster before the judge, the judge would devour him. Yet when they came before the judge to make their arguments, the hawk grabbed the rooster who had originally brought the case to court. The rooster shouted, 'Not me! You want the one who is running away!' The hawk replied, 'Abandon all hope of being released from my talons today! Justice demands that you yourself should suffer the punishment that you intended for another.'
ground_truth_moral: Those who scheme against others may ultimately fall victim to their own plots.

summaries.direct_moral: He who plots another's destruction often falls into his own trap.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: He who seeks to bring ruin upon others often brings that same ruin upon himself.
summaries.proverb: He who digs a pit for another falls into it himself.
summaries.cot_proverb: He who digs a pit for others falls into it himself.

fable_text: A nightingale, sitting aloft upon an oak and singing according to his wont, was seen by a Hawk who, being in need of food, swooped down and seized him. The Nightingale, about to lose his life, earnestly begged the Hawk to let him go, saying that he was not big enough to satisfy the hunger of a Hawk who, if he wanted food, ought to pursue the larger birds. The Hawk, interrupting him, said: 'I should indeed have lost my senses if I should let go food ready in my hand, for the sake of pursuing birds which are not yet even within sight.'
ground_truth_moral: Do not neglect small advantages in hopes of better ones.

summaries.direct_moral: A bird in the hand is worth two in the bush.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: It is unwise to abandon a certain profit for the pursuit of a larger but uncertain prospect.
summaries.proverb: A bird in the hand is worth two in the bush.
summaries.cot_proverb: A bird in the hand is worth two in the bush.

fable_text: There was a widow who had a hen that laid one egg each and every day. The woman then began to feed the hen more lavishly, thinking that if the hen ate more grain she would lay two eggs. The hen got so fat from eating all the food that she stopped laying eggs entirely.
ground_truth_moral: People who grasp at more than they need lose the little that they held in their hands.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed often leads to the loss of what one already possesses.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Greed often leads to the loss of what one already possesses.
summaries.proverb: Grasp all, lose all.
summaries.cot_proverb: Greed often loses all in seeking for more.

fable_text: A hen came across the eggs of a snake and devoted herself to them, settling atop the eggs and brooding on them. A swallow saw what the hen was doing and said, 'O you stupid, senseless creature! They will destroy you first of all and then destroy everyone around you!'
ground_truth_moral: We should never put our trust in a wicked man, even if he seems to be completely innocuous.

summaries.direct_moral: Nurturing the wicked only ensures your own destruction.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Nurturing a threat inevitably leads to one's own destruction.
summaries.proverb: He who nurtures evil invites his own destruction.
summaries.cot_proverb: He who nurtures the wicked brings about his own destruction.

fable_text: Hermes wanted to know how much people valued him, so he assumed a human form and went into a sculptor's workshop. He saw there a statue of Zeus and he asked how much it cost. The man said that it cost a drachma. Hermes smiled, and asked how much the statue of Hera would be. The man named a still higher price. When Hermes saw a statue of himself, he expected that he would be reckoned at an even higher price, since he delivered the messages of the gods and brought profit to mankind. But when he asked how much the statue of Hermes would cost, the sculptor replied, 'If you buy those other two, I'll throw this one in for free!'
ground_truth_moral: Vanity can blind one to their true worth in the eyes of others.

summaries.direct_moral: Self-importance often leads to a humbling realization of one's true value to others.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Vanity often leads to a humbling realization of one's true insignificance in the eyes of others.
summaries.proverb: Pride goes before a fall.
summaries.cot_proverb: Vanity often leads to humiliation.

fable_text: After Zeus had fashioned the human race, he ordered Hermes to give them intelligence. Hermes divided intelligence into equal portions and then applied it to each person. The result was that short people became wise, since they were more completely suffused with the standard dose of intelligence, while the tall people turned out stupid, since the potion that was poured into their bodies did not even reach as high as their knees.
ground_truth_moral: Physical stature does not guarantee wisdom or intelligence.

summaries.direct_moral: A large body is no guarantee of a great mind.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Inner quality is often more concentrated in modest forms than in large ones.
summaries.proverb: Wisdom is not measured by stature.
summaries.cot_proverb: A large body is no guarantee of a great mind.

fable_text: A man was chopping wood by a certain river when he dropped his axe and it was carried away by the current. The man then sat down on the riverbank and began to weep. The god Hermes finally took pity on the man and appeared before him. When Hermes learned the reason for his sorrow, he brought up a golden axe and asked whether that was the man's axe. The man said that it was not his. A second time, Hermes brought up a silver axe, and again asked the man if this was the axe he had lost but the man said that it was not. The third time Hermes brought up the axe that the man had lost and when the man recognized his axe, Hermes rewarded the man's honesty by giving all of the axes to him as a gift. The man took the axes and went to tell his friends what had happened. One of the men was jealous and wanted to do the same thing, so he took his axe and went to the river. He began chopping some wood and then intentionally let his axe fall into the whirling waters. As he was weeping, Hermes appeared and asked him what had happened, and the man said that he had lost his axe. When Hermes brought up the golden axe and asked the man if that was the axe he had lost, the greedy man got excited and said that it was the one. Not only did the man fail to receive any gifts from the god, he didn't even retrieve his own axe.
ground_truth_moral: Honesty is the best policy.

summaries.direct_moral: Honesty is rewarded, whereas greed and deceit lead to ruin.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Honesty is rewarded, while greed leads to the loss of even that which one already possesses.
summaries.proverb: Honesty is the best policy.
summaries.cot_proverb: Honesty is the best policy.

fable_text: A traveller who needed to make a long journey vowed that if he found anything, he would give half of it to Hermes. When he came across a bag full of dates and almonds he grabbed the bag and ate the almonds and dates. He then placed the pits of the dates and the shells of the almonds upon an altar and said 'You have what was promised you, O Hermes: I have saved the outsides and the insides for you!'
ground_truth_moral: The promises of the greedy are often empty and insincere.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed often leads people to use clever technicalities to avoid fulfilling the spirit of their promises.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Greed often prompts people to use clever arguments to evade their moral or spiritual obligations.
summaries.proverb: Greed knows no honor.
summaries.cot_proverb: Greed seeks to cheat even the gods.

fable_text: A man fashioned a Hermes out of wood and carried it to the market to put it up for sale but no customers approached him. In order to attract some buyers the man began to shout that he was selling a wish-fulfilling god who brought profit to its owner. 'Hey you,' someone said, 'why are you putting such a thing up for sale, instead of enjoying its benefits yourself?' The man answered, 'I am in need of some immediate benefits, but this god happens to take his time when distributing profits!'
ground_truth_moral: When you lie, you often have to expand the lie.

summaries.direct_moral: Immediate necessity often outweighs the promise of future prosperity.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Immediate necessity often takes precedence over the promise of future or uncertain gains.
summaries.proverb: Trust not a man who promises wealth while he himself is in want.
summaries.cot_proverb: Never trust a man who promises to provide for others what he cannot provide for himself.

fable_text: A man kept a hero shrine in his house and made expensive sacrifices to it. Since he was constantly paying out money and spending great sums on the offerings, the god came to him one night and said: 'Look here, stop squandering your wealth: if you spend everything and become poor, you'll put the blame on me!'
ground_truth_moral: When people suffer misfortune because of their own foolishness, they often blame the gods.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not blame others for the misfortunes caused by your own lack of moderation.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: People often blame external forces for the misfortunes caused by their own lack of moderation.
summaries.proverb: Live within your means.
summaries.cot_proverb: Blame not the gods for the consequences of your own folly.

fable_text: The river saw that a cowhide was being swept along by his current, so he said to her, 'What is your name?' The cowhide answered, 'They call me Tough. ' Splashing the hide with his waves, the river said, 'You better get yourself another name, since I am going to soften you up!'
ground_truth_moral: Life's disasters can often bring boastful and presumptuous people back down to earth.

summaries.direct_moral: Even the toughest resistance eventually yields to persistent force.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Persistent force eventually overcomes the most stubborn resistance.
summaries.proverb: Persistence wears away resistance.
summaries.cot_proverb: Persistence overcomes even the toughest resistance.

fable_text: A mountain had gone into labour and was groaning terribly. Such rumours excited great expectations all over the country. In the end, however, the mountain gave birth to a mouse.
ground_truth_moral: Don't promise big things if you can't deliver.

summaries.direct_moral: Great expectations often yield insignificant results.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Boastful preparations and grand promises often result in insignificant outcomes.
summaries.proverb: Much ado about nothing.
summaries.cot_proverb: Much ado about nothing.

fable_text: A certain man took a deposit from a friend but intended to keep it for himself. When the depositor then summoned him to swear an oath regarding the deposit, he realized the danger he was in and prepared to leave the city and go to his farm. When he reached the city gates, he saw a lame man who was also on his way out of town. He asked the man who he was and where he was going. The man said that he was the god named Oath and that he was on his way to track down wicked people. The man then asked Oath how often he revisited each city. Oath replied, 'I come back after forty years, or sometimes thirty.' Accordingly, on the very next day the man did not hesitate to swear an oath that he had never received the deposit. But then the man ran into Oath, who dragged him off to the edge of a cliff. The man asked Oath how he could have said that he wasn't coming back for another thirty years when in fact he didn't even grant him a single day's reprieve. Oath explained, 'You also need to know that if somebody intends to provoke me, I am accustomed to come back again the very same day.'
ground_truth_moral: There is no fixed day on which wicked people are punished.

summaries.direct_moral: Justice for the wicked is unpredictable and often strikes far swifter than anticipated.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Divine retribution falls most swiftly upon those who deliberately attempt to exploit its perceived delay.
summaries.proverb: Justice strikes swiftest when provoked.
summaries.cot_proverb: Punishment follows close on the heels of the crime.

fable_text: As he was crossing through a field, a wolf found some barley. Since wolves don't eat barley, he ignored it and continued on his way. The wolf then ran into a horse. He led the horse into the field and showed him the barley, saying that instead of eating the barley himself, he had saved it for the horse, since he liked to hear the sound of the horse's teeth grinding together. The horse then said to the wolf, 'Look here, if you wolves ate barley, you would never have put the pleasure of your ears before your stomach!'
ground_truth_moral: Nobody believes people who are inherently wicked, even if they pretend to be good-natured.

summaries.direct_moral: There is no merit in giving away what you cannot use yourself.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True generosity is measured by sacrifice rather than the convenient disposal of what one cannot use.
summaries.proverb: There is no merit in giving away what you cannot use.
summaries.cot_proverb: It is easy to be generous with what you cannot use.

fable_text: A certain hunter saw a wolf attacking a flock and tearing to pieces as many of the sheep as he could. The hunter skilfully hunted down the wolf and set his dogs on him. Then he shouted at the wolf, 'O you terrible beast, where is that former strength of yours? You cannot even withstand the attack of these dogs!'
ground_truth_moral: Each man makes his reputation in the field which is his specialty.

summaries.direct_moral: It is easy to be fierce against the defenseless, but true strength is proven only against an equal.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Those who are bold in oppressing the weak are often revealed as cowards when confronted by the strong.
summaries.proverb: Cowards are bold only against the defenseless.
summaries.cot_proverb: A bully is a coward when he meets his match.

fable_text: There was a boy singing sweet serenades in the middle of the night. A woman heard him and got up from bed to peep out the window at him. When she saw the boy, who looked very beautiful in the shining moonlight, she left her husband asleep and went downstairs and out the door. She then met the boy in the street and satisfied herself completely. All of a sudden her husband woke up and wanted to find out where his wife had gone. Not finding her inside the house, he didn't just stand there gaping but instead followed her outside and said, 'It's alright. Go ahead and persuade the boy to sleep in our house.' So he took the boy and brought him inside. He didn't have any trouble after that, and joined in whenever the two of them wanted to do something.
ground_truth_moral: Acceptance can lead to unexpected harmony.

summaries.direct_moral: Acceptance and participation are often more beneficial than jealousy and conflict.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Pragmatic compromise can transform potential conflict into mutual benefit.
summaries.proverb: If you can’t beat them, join them.
summaries.cot_proverb: If you can't beat them, join them.

fable_text: They say that the hyena has a double nature: for a period of time the hyena is male, and then later on she is female. The story goes that when a male hyena was treating a female badly, she said to him, 'Listen here: remember how things used to be, and don't forget that I will be a male hyena the next time around!'
ground_truth_moral: People who have been judged in the past can later on be in a position to judge their former teachers.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not mistreat others, for positions of power may soon be reversed.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Treat others with the same fairness you would expect if your roles were reversed.
summaries.proverb: Do as you would be done by, for roles may soon be reversed.
summaries.cot_proverb: Treat your inferiors as you would have your superiors treat you.

fable_text: A man caught a jackdaw and tied the bird's foot with a piece of string so that he could give the bird to his children as a present. The jackdaw, however, could not stand to live in human society, so when they let him loose for just a moment, he ran away. But when he got back to his nest, the string became entangled in the branches, so that the jackdaw was unable to fly. As he was dying, the bird said to himself, 'How stupid of me! Since I could not stand being a slave in human society, I have brought about my own death.'
ground_truth_moral: It is not the place, nor the condition, but the mind alone that is responsible for our happiness.

summaries.direct_moral: In seeking to avoid a minor misfortune, one may fall into a greater disaster.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: In seeking to escape a lesser misfortune through impulsive action, one often falls into a far greater disaster.
summaries.proverb: Out of the frying pan, into the fire.
summaries.cot_proverb: Out of the frying pan into the fire.

fable_text: One day the lion pretended to be sick and went limping up to the unicorn, his chief enemy. He greeted the unicorn and said to him, 'Let us put aside all that we have done in the past, because I am no longer able to harm anyone at all. As you can see, I am old and suffering from various ailments. But before I die, I would very much like to speak with my wife who is out in the desert. So, kind sir, if I might be so bold, I would like to borrow your horn to use as a walking stick on my journey since it is just the right length and very sturdy. I promise to return it to you as soon as I reach my wife; I give you my word.' The unicorn believed the lion and pitied his feigned distress, so he loaned the lion his horn and was thus left defenceless. The lion then inflicted a serious wound on the unicorn and laid him low. The unicorn said, 'You are guilty not so much of cruelty as of treachery, since you repaid my favour with wickedness and betrayed the promise you made me.' The lion said, 'You fool, don't you know that the saying: The man who prolongs his enemy's life takes something from his own; clemency does not entail showing mercy to one's enemies.' The unicorn replied, 'You traitor, don't you know that in the same book it is written: Let the victory which we contrive by the sword be an honourable victory or no victory at all; let not posterity read that I won by guile; no perfidy should obscure my triumph.'
ground_truth_moral: Do not ever trust your enemy. Always protect yourself from him, even if he comes to you humble and supplicating.

summaries.direct_moral: Never trust the feigned weakness of an enemy or surrender your means of defense.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Misplaced compassion for a treacherous enemy leads to the surrender of one's own safety and certain ruin.
summaries.proverb: Never trust the word of a known enemy.
summaries.cot_proverb: Never trust the promises of a reconciled enemy.

fable_text: A kid had wandered off from the flock and was being chased by a wolf. Unable to get away, the kid fell into the wolf's clutches so she turned to him and said, 'I know for a fact that I am about to become your dinner, but I would like to die with dignity: please play the flute for me so that I can do a little dance.' The wolf played the flute and the kid danced to the music. The sound alerted the shepherd's dogs who attacked the wolf and chased him away. As the wolf ran off he said to himself, 'It serves me right for trying to be a musician instead of a butcher!'
ground_truth_moral: Do not let anything turn you from your purpose.

summaries.direct_moral: Stick to your own business and avoid being distracted by tasks for which you are unsuited.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Do not lose sight of your primary purpose by attempting roles for which you are not suited.
summaries.proverb: Stick to your trade.
summaries.cot_proverb: Stick to your trade.

fable_text: The Goat, going abroad to feed, shut up her young Kid at borne, charging him to bolt the door fast, and open it to nobody, till she herself should return. The Wolf, who lay lurking just by, heard this charge given; and soon after came and knocked at the door, counterfeiting the voice of the Goat, and desiring to be admitted. The Kid, looking out at a window, and finding the cheat, bid him go about his business; for, however he might imitate a Goat's voice, yet he appeared too much like a Wolf to be trusted.
ground_truth_moral: A hypocrite can usually be found out.

summaries.direct_moral: Caution is the best safeguard against deception.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Vigilance and discernment are the surest safeguards against those who hide their malice behind a mask of deception.
summaries.proverb: Trust your eyes rather than your ears.
summaries.cot_proverb: Caution is the parent of safety.

fable_text: A seagull having bolted down too large a fish, burst its deep gullet-bag and lay down on the shore to die. A Kite saw him and exclaimed: 'You richly deserve your fate; for a bird of the air has no business to seek its food from the sea.'
ground_truth_moral: Every man should be content to mind his own business.

summaries.direct_moral: Stick to your own domain and avoid overreaching your natural limits.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Every person should be content to remain within their own proper sphere of activity.
summaries.proverb: Stay in your own element.
summaries.cot_proverb: Every man should stick to his own sphere.

fable_text: A wolf had been mauled by dogs and had cast himself down on the ground. Because of his injuries, the wolf could not go in search of food, so when he saw a sheep, he begged her to bring him something to drink from the river that ran nearby. 'If you just give me something to drink,' said the wolf, 'I will find myself something to eat.' The sheep replied, 'But if I give you with something to drink, then you will make me your dinner as well!'
ground_truth_moral: Hypocritical speeches are easily seen through.

summaries.direct_moral: Beware the deceptive pleas of a natural enemy.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Do not trust the entreaties of a known enemy, regardless of their apparent distress.
summaries.proverb: Trust not the promises of an enemy.
summaries.cot_proverb: A villain's plea is a trap for the unwary.

fable_text: Thieves broke into a certain house and didn't find anything inside except a rooster. The thieves grabbed the rooster and made their escape. Later, when they were ready to kill him, the rooster begged the thieves to let him go, claiming that he was useful to people because he woke them to go about their tasks in the dark. The thieves said, 'All the more reason to kill you: when you wake them up, you prevent us from robbing their houses!'
ground_truth_moral: The safeguards of virtue are hateful to those with evil intentions.

summaries.direct_moral: What serves the public good is often a threat to the wicked.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The very virtues that benefit society are often the reasons why the wicked seek to eliminate them.
summaries.proverb: What serves the honest hinders the wicked.
summaries.cot_proverb: What is a merit to the good is a vice to the wicked.

fable_text: A pigeon had grown very thirsty, so she flew from place to place looking for some water to drink. She saw a water jar painted on a wall and thought that it was actually full of water, so she flew right into the wall in order to take a drink. As the pigeon collided with the wall, she brought her life abruptly to an end. As she drew her last breath, the pigeon said to herself, 'What a wretched and unlucky creature I am! I didn't even suspect that looking for something to drink could bring about my own demise.'
ground_truth_moral: Caution is far superior to reckless haste and hurry.

summaries.direct_moral: Excessive zeal without discernment leads to disaster.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Acting on hasty impulses driven by intense desire often leads to self-destruction.
summaries.proverb: Look before you leap.
summaries.cot_proverb: Look before you leap.

fable_text: A lark fell into a snare and sang a lament, 'Woe is me, wretched and unlucky bird that I am! I have brought about my own demise not for gold or silver or some object of value, but merely for a tiny bit of food.'
ground_truth_moral: People are willing to risk their lives for the sake of some petty profit.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not risk your life for a trivial gain.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: It is a great folly to risk one's life or freedom for the sake of a trivial reward.
summaries.proverb: Small gains often bring great losses.
summaries.cot_proverb: A small gain is not worth a great risk.

fable_text: A Lark, caught in a snare, pleaded earnestly with the Fowler for her life. 'What have I done that I must die?' said she; 'I have stolen neither gold nor silver, but only a grain of corn to satisfy my hunger.' The Man, without deigning any reply, twisted her neck and threw her into his sack.
ground_truth_moral: When your adversary is also your judge, don't bother arguing.

summaries.direct_moral: Innocence is no shield against a ruthless enemy.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Might often overrides mercy regardless of the insignificance of the offense.
summaries.proverb: Small faults are often punished as severely as great crimes.
summaries.cot_proverb: Cruelty is deaf to the voice of reason.

fable_text: There were two men travelling together: one was a liar and the other always told the truth. Their journey led them to the land of the monkeys. There was a whole crowd of monkeys there and one of them noticed the travellers. The monkey who was clearly their leader ordered that the men be detained. Since he wanted to know what the men thought of him, he commanded all rest of the monkeys to stand before him in a long line to his right and to his left, while a seat was prepared for him to sit on (this monkey had once seen the emperor, so he was ordering his monkeys to line up for him in the same way). The men were then told to come forward into the midst of the monkeys. The chief monkey said, 'Who am I?' The liar said, 'You are the emperor!' Then the monkey asked, 'And those whom you see standing before me: who are they?' The man answered, 'They are your noble companions, your chancellors, your officials and the commanders of your armies!' Because these lies flattered the monkey and his troops, he ordered that the man be showered with presents. All the monkeys were fooled by his flattery. Meanwhile, the man who always told the truth thought to himself, 'If that liar received such rewards for telling lies, then surely I will receive an even greater reward for telling the truth.' The chief monkey said to the second man, 'Now you tell me who I am, and who are these whom you see standing before me?' And the man who always loved the truth and never lied said to the monkey, 'You are simply a monkey, and all of these similar simians are monkeys as well!' The chief monkey immediately ordered the monkeys to attack the man with their teeth and claws because he had spoken the truth.
ground_truth_moral: Those who thrive on deception often retaliate against those who speak the truth.

summaries.direct_moral: Flattery often earns rewards while the truth invites punishment from the vain.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Vanity often rewards a pleasing lie while punishing an unwelcome truth.
summaries.proverb: Truth breeds hatred; flattery wins friends.
summaries.cot_proverb: Flattery earns rewards, while the truth brings trouble.

fable_text: Some field-mice were playing in the woods where a lion was sleeping when one of the mice accidentally ran over the lion. The lion woke up and immediately grabbed the wretched little mouse with his paw. The mouse begged for mercy, since he had not meant to do the lion any harm. The lion decided that to kill such a tiny creature would be a cause for reproach rather than glory, so he forgave the mouse and let him go. A few days later, the lion fell into a pit and was trapped. He started to roar, and when the mouse heard him, he came running. Recognizing the lion in the trap, the mouse said to him, 'I have not forgotten the kindness that you showed me!' The mouse then began to gnaw at the cords binding the lion, cutting through the strands and undoing the clever ingenuity of the hunter's art. The mouse was thus able to restore the lion to the woods, setting him free from his captivity.
ground_truth_moral: No act of kindness, no matter how small, is ever wasted

summaries.direct_moral: No act of kindness, no matter how small, is ever wasted.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Acts of kindness are never wasted, as even the smallest among us may one day repay a great favor.
summaries.proverb: No act of kindness is ever wasted.
summaries.cot_proverb: No act of kindness, no matter how small, is ever wasted.

fable_text: A herdsman tending his flock in a forest lost a Bull-calf from the fold. After a long and fruitless search, he made a vow that, if he could only discover the thief who had stolen the Calf, he would offer a lamb in sacrifice to Hermes, Pan, and the Guardian Deities of the forest. Not long afterwards, as he ascended a small hillock, he saw at its foot a Lion feeding on the Calf. Terrified at the sight, he lifted his eyes and his hands to heaven, and said: 'Just now I vowed to offer a lamb to the Guardian Deities of the forest if I could only find out who had robbed me; but now that I have discovered the thief, I would willingly add a full-grown Bull to the Calf I have lost, if I may only secure my own escape from him in safety.''
ground_truth_moral: Be careful what you wish for; your wish may be granted.

summaries.direct_moral: We often seek that which we would most fear to find.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The fulfillment of a wish can often bring more terror than the misfortune that prompted it.
summaries.proverb: Be careful what you wish for.
summaries.cot_proverb: Be careful what you wish for, lest you should find it.

fable_text: A wolf, having stolen a lamb from a fold, was carrying him off to his lair. A Lion met him in the path, and seizing the lamb, took it from him. Standing at a safe distance, the Wolf exclaimed, 'You have unrighteously taken that which was mine from me!' To which the Lion jeeringly replied, 'It was righteously yours, eh? The gift of a friend?'
ground_truth_moral: Things you acquire through evil means can be taken from you by evil means.

summaries.direct_moral: One cannot rightfully complain of being treated as they treat others.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Those who gain through injustice have no right to complain when they are treated in kind.
summaries.proverb: Ill-gotten gains never prosper.
summaries.cot_proverb: What is evil-won is evil-lost.

fable_text: There is a tale of a lizard in a quest to match the length of a snake. In its effort to equal the snake's size, the lizard burst into pieces right down the middle.
ground_truth_moral: You will hurt yourself and accomplish nothing if you try to imitate someone who is far better than you are.

summaries.direct_moral: Attempting to exceed one's natural limits leads to self-destruction.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Attempting to imitate those far beyond one's natural capacity leads to self-destruction.
summaries.proverb: Know your measure.
summaries.cot_proverb: He who strives to be what he is not brings about his own destruction.

fable_text: They say that in the beginning, when the animals were being formed, they received their endowments from Zeus. To some he gave strength, and to some speed, and to others wings. Man, however, was still naked so he said to Zeus, 'I am the only one that you have left without a gift.'
