fable_text: A gnat came and said to a Lion, 'I do not in the least fear you, nor are you stronger than I am. For in what does your strength consist? You can scratch with your claws and bite with your teeth as a woman in her quarrels. I repeat that I am altogether more powerful than you; and if you doubt it, let us fight and see who will conquer.' The Gnat, having sounded his horn, fastened himself upon the Lion and stung him on the nostrils and the parts of the face devoid of hair. While trying to crush him, the Lion tore himself with his claws, until he punished himself severely. The Gnat thus prevailed over the Lion, and, buzzing about in a song of triumph, flew away. But shortly afterwards he became entangled in the meshes of a cobweb and was eaten by a spider. He greatly lamented his fate, saying, 'Woe is me! that I, who can wage war successfully with the hugest beasts, should perish myself from this spider, the most inconsiderable of insects!'
ground_truth_moral: No matter how you brag, you can be undone.

summaries.direct_moral: Pride in a great victory often leads to ruin by a small danger.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The greatest triumphs are often followed by ruin when one overlooks the smallest dangers through overconfidence.
summaries.proverb: Pride goes before a fall.
summaries.cot_proverb: Pride goes before a fall.

fable_text: Roaming by the mountainside at sundown, a Wolf saw his own shadow become greatly extended and magnified, and he said to himself, 'Why should I, being of such an immense size and extending nearly an acre in length, be afraid of the Lion? Ought I not to be acknowledged as King of all the collected beasts?' While he was indulging in these proud thoughts, a Lion fell upon him and killed him. He exclaimed with a too late repentance, 'Wretched me! this overestimation of myself is the cause of my destruction.'
ground_truth_moral: Do not let your fancy make you forget realities.

summaries.direct_moral: Pride based on self-delusion leads to destruction.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-conceit based on false appearances leads to ruin.
summaries.proverb: Pride goes before a fall.
summaries.cot_proverb: Pride goes before a fall.

fable_text: A solitary sheep suddenly caught sight of a wolf and fled into the sheepfold, which by chance had been left open since there was a sacrifice was being readied for the holiday. The wolf did not come within the walls of the sheepfold but stood outside and tried to win the sheep's trust. 'Don't you see that altar there covered with blood?' said the wolf. 'Come out, or else they will seize you and sacrifice you too.' The sheep replied, 'Don't you worry about my place of refuge; I'm doing just fine. And even if things turn out as you say, I would rather become an offering for the god than a meal for a wolf!'
ground_truth_moral: Better to face a known danger than to trust a deceitful promise of safety.

summaries.direct_moral: A noble death is preferable to a shameful one.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: An honorable end is preferable to falling prey to a treacherous enemy.
summaries.proverb: Better a noble death than a base one.
summaries.cot_proverb: Better to die for a noble cause than to satisfy a wicked enemy.

fable_text: A wall who was being gouged by a stake exclaimed, 'Hey! Why are you making this gash in me, when I never did you any harm?' The stake answered, 'It's not my fault! Blame the man who is pounding me so hard from behind.'
ground_truth_moral: Look at the source when placing the blame.

summaries.direct_moral: Blame should fall on the instigator of an action rather than the instrument used to perform it.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Responsibility for an action belongs to the primary agent rather than the instrument employed to carry it out.
summaries.proverb: Blame the doer, not the instrument.
summaries.cot_proverb: Blame not the tool, but the hand that wields it.

fable_text: Winter made fun of Spring and mocked her for the fact that as soon as spring appears, nobody can keep still: some people go off to the meadows or into the woods, others like to gather flowers and lilies or perhaps to gaze upon a rose as they twirl it in the air or to twine it in their hair; while some board ships and even cross the sea to meet different kinds of people; no one worries any longer about the winds or the great downpours of rain from the sky. 'Whereas I resemble a dictator or a despot,' said Winter. 'I command everyone to look not at the sky but down toward the ground; I frighten them and make them tremble and sometimes I make them content themselves while having to stay indoors all day.' Spring replied, 'Indeed, that is exactly why mankind would be glad to get rid of you, whereas even the mere mention of my name is enough to bring them pleasure. By Zeus, there is no name more pleasant than mine! That is why they think fondly of me when I am gone and give thanks when I appear again.'
ground_truth_moral: It is better to be loved than feared.

summaries.direct_moral: It is better to be loved for bringing joy than to be feared for imposing hardship.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True influence and lasting affection are earned through benevolence and the inspiration of joy rather than through fear and authoritarian control.
summaries.proverb: Better to be loved than feared.
summaries.cot_proverb: Gentleness wins more hearts than severity.

fable_text: An Arab camel-driver, after completing the loading of his Camel, asked him which he would like best, to go up hill or down. The poor beast replied, not without a touch of reason: 'Why do you ask me? Is it that the level way through the desert is closed?'
ground_truth_moral: Foresake the hard when an easier way is available.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not choose between two hardships when a simpler alternative exists.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: One should seek the most efficient course rather than choosing between unnecessary hardships.
summaries.proverb: The level way is best.
summaries.cot_proverb: The middle path is best.

fable_text: A Husbandman, busily employed in trenching his Vineyard, laid down for awhile the Mattock he was using. When he went to take it up again, it was gone. He called together all his hired men, and asked them if they had seen the tool. They all denied any knowledge of it; and the Man, in a great rage, said he knew that one of them must have taken it, and, let it cost him what it might, he would find out the thief. With that view he insisted upon their going with him to the shrine of a famous oracle in a neighbouring city. Arrived within the city gates, they stopped at the fountain in the market-place, to bathe their feet. Just at that moment the town-crier came up, and in a loud voice announced that, the sacred shrine having been robbed last night, he was told to offer a large reward to any one who could discover the thief. Thereupon the Husbandman at once called upon his men to turn their faces homewards. 'If this god,' said he, 'cannot tell who has robbed his temple, the chances are that he knows as little who has taken my Mattock.'
ground_truth_moral: Even gods may not know everything.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not seek help from those who cannot solve their own problems.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: He who cannot help himself is unfit to help others.
summaries.proverb: He who cannot help himself cannot help others.
summaries.cot_proverb: He who cannot help himself is ill-qualified to help others.

fable_text: A philosopher witnessed from the shore the shipwreck of a vessel, of which the crew and passengers were all drowned. He inveighed against the injustice of Providence, which would for the sake of one criminal perchance sailing in the ship allow so many innocent persons to perish. As he was indulging in these reflections, he found himself surrounded by a whole army of Ants, near whose nest he was standing. One of them climbed up and stung him, and he immediately trampled them all to death with his foot. Mercury presented himself, and striking the Philosopher with his wand, said, 'And are you indeed to make yourself a judge of the dealings of Providence, who hast thyself in a similar manner treated these poor Ants?'
ground_truth_moral: Act as you profess to believe.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not condemn in others the very faults you commit yourself.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: One should not condemn in others the same faults that they manifest in their own actions.
summaries.proverb: Condemn not in others what you practice yourself.
summaries.cot_proverb: Judge not others by a standard you do not yourself observe.

fable_text: There was a four-cornered statue of Hermes by the side of the road, with a heap of stones piled at its base. A dog approached the statue and said to it, 'To begin with, Hermes, I salute you! And now I am going to anoint you, since I cannot let a god go by without anointing him, much less a god of the athletes.' Hermes said to the dog, 'If you can just leave the oil alone and not pee on me, I shall be grateful enough; you do not need to honour me in any other way!'
ground_truth_moral: Respect is often better shown by refraining from disrespect.

summaries.direct_moral: Unwelcome honor is merely an insult in disguise.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Abstaining from insult is a more meaningful tribute than offering unwanted or inappropriate honors.
summaries.proverb: Better no honor than a shameful one.
summaries.cot_proverb: Refrain from doing harm, and you have done enough.

fable_text: Zeus ordered Hermes to write down people's sins and wicked deeds on potsherds and to pile them in a designated box, so that Zeus could then peruse them and exact a penalty from each person as appropriate. Given that the potsherds are all piled up one on top of the other until the moment that Zeus examines them, he gets to some of them quite soon while others have to wait.
ground_truth_moral: Sometimes, Wicked people commit crime in haste but are not punished until much later.

summaries.direct_moral: Punishment for wicked deeds is inevitable, even if its arrival is delayed.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The timing of retribution is unpredictable, but its eventual arrival is certain.
summaries.proverb: The wheels of justice turn slowly, but surely.
summaries.cot_proverb: Justice is slow but sure.

fable_text: Once upon a time, the Sun wanted to get married but the frogs raised a cry of protest up to the heavens. Jupiter, disturbed by their shouting, asked the reason for their complaint, and one of the swamp's inhabitants explained, 'Already one Sun is enough to burn up all the ponds, condemning us to a miserable death in our parched abode. What is going to happen to us when he will have sons of his own?'
ground_truth_moral: Watch the actions of your enemy.

summaries.direct_moral: One great burden is enough without its multiplication.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: One should dread the potential multiplication of a force that is already destructive.
summaries.proverb: Foresee the consequences of a change.
summaries.cot_proverb: One bad master is enough for any man.

fable_text: A Soldier cared for his Horse. So long as there was war, he fed him carefully and treated him as almost an equal. But, when the war ended, the Soldier only gave him chaff to eat and made him carry heavy loads; he treated the Horse badly. After awhile, war was again declared and both Soldier and Horse were called to duty. The Soldier put military trappings on the horse and mounted clad in a coat of mail. The Horse immediately fell down under the weight. He said to his master: 'You must now go to war on foot, for you have changed me from a Horse to an Ass and you cannot expect me to change back in a moment.'
ground_truth_moral: Care defines ability.

summaries.direct_moral: Neglect in times of peace leads to failure in times of need.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Neglect in times of security ensures failure in times of need.
summaries.proverb: Neglect in peace leads to failure in war.
summaries.cot_proverb: In times of peace, prepare for war.

fable_text: A dog who was being trained to fight in the public games snapped the collar around his neck and went dashing through the streets. The street dogs could tell that this fellow had been living the good life since he was as stout as a bull, so they asked him why he was running away. The runaway replied, 'It's true that I have been enjoying an abundance of food and living a comfortable life, but I am also forced to fight with lions and bears, risking my life in the arena.' The other dogs then said to one another, 'Our poor life must be very fine, because we don't have to fight with lions and bears!'
ground_truth_moral: A humble life free from danger is more valuable than a life of luxury filled with peril.

summaries.direct_moral: Humble safety is better than dangerous luxury.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: A modest life in safety is preferable to a luxurious life in danger.
summaries.proverb: Better a crust in peace than a feast in fear.
summaries.cot_proverb: A crust in peace is better than a feast in fear.

fable_text: The beasts of the field and forest had a Lion as their king. He was neither wrathful, cruel, nor tyrannical, but just and gentle as a king could be. During his reign he made a royal proclamation for a general assembly of all the birds and beasts, and drew up conditions for a universal league, in which the Wolf and the Lamb, the Panther and the Kid, the Tiger and the Stag, the Dog and the Hare, should live together in perfect peace and amity. The Hare said, 'Oh, how I have longed to see this day, in which the weak shall take their place with impunity by the side of the strong.' And after the Hare said this, he ran for his life.
ground_truth_moral: Nature reveals itself.

summaries.direct_moral: Proclamations of peace cannot override the inherent dangers that the strong pose to the weak.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: No decree of peace can eliminate the inherent danger that the powerful pose to the vulnerable.
summaries.proverb: Nature is not changed by decree.
summaries.cot_proverb: Fair words do not make the weak feel safe in the company of the strong.

fable_text: There was a lion who had fallen ill and was lying in a stony ravine, his sluggish limbs stretched out upon the ground. A friendly fox kept him company, and one day the lion said to her, 'I suppose you want me to survive, so listen: I've got a craving for the deer who lives in that dense thicket of pines there in the wilds of the forest. At the moment I no longer have the strength to go hunting after deer myself, but if you would agree to lay a trap with that honeyed speech of yours, the deer could be within my grasp.' The sly fox went off and found the deer in the wild woodlands, gambolling in a meadow of tender grass. The fox prostrated herself before the deer and greeted her, saying that she had come to relay some auspicious information. 'As you know,' the fox said, 'the lion is my neighbour, but he is very sick and about to die, so he has been thinking about who will be king of the beasts after he is gone. The boar is an idiot, the bear is lazy, the leopard is impulsive, the tiger is a loner who keeps to himself... but he thinks that the deer would make a most worthy ruler, since she has an impressive appearance and lives a long time. And the antlers of the deer can scare away all kinds of snakes, why, the antlers of the deer are like trees, not at all like the horns of a bull! Need I say more? You have been duly elected: you will rule over the beasts of the hills. When that finally happens, O Mistress, remember that it was the fox who was the first to inform you. That is why I came here, and now good-bye, my dear. I need to hurry back to the lion so that he won't be looking for me again; he relies on my advice in absolutely everything. And I think it would be good if you also obeyed that venerable old head. You need to come to his bedside and comfort him in his trouble. Even little things can sway the thoughts of those who are in the last hours of life; the souls of the dying can be seen in their eyes.' This is what the sly fox said to the deer, and the deer's heart swelled at the sound of those deceitful words. She came to the hollow cave of the beast, with no idea of what lay in store for her. The lion recklessly sprang up from his bed and launched a hasty attack, but he only managed to slash the deer's ears with his pointed claws as the wretched creature ran straight out the door and disappeared into the depths of the woods. The fox wrung her hands in frustration, since her efforts had proved utterly futile. As for the lion, he groaned and chewed at thin air, equally beset by both hunger and despair. Once again he summoned the fox and asked her to find yet another trick to use to catch the deer. The fox plumbed the very depths of her cunning and then said, 'This is a difficult task indeed. But nevertheless I will carry out your command!' The fox then set off after the deer, keen as a hound on the trail, devising elaborate traps and all kinds of mischief. Whenever she ran into a shepherd, the fox would ask if he had happened to see a bleeding deer on the run. And when the shepherd had indeed caught a glimpse of the deer, he would point the fox in the right direction. She finally found the deer concealed in the shade, where she had stopped to catch her breath. The fox stood and stared at the deer, eyebrows raised, the very incarnation of shamelessness. A shiver ran down the deer's spine and her legs quivered as she angrily said to the fox, 'Oh you abominable creature! If you dare to come near me or utter so much as a single word, you will live to regret it! Go find some other simpletons that you can outfox; pick someone else to be king and put him on the throne!' But the fox was undaunted and said to the deer, 'Can you really be so mean spirited? So overcome by fear? So suspicious of your friends? The lion only wanted what was good for you! In an attempt to rouse you from your former idleness, he tugged at your ear, as a father might do on his deathbed. He wanted to bestow on you every precept you would need in order to take charge of such a kingdom, but you could not even withstand the touch of his feeble hand! Instead, you violently turned aside, inflicting a serious wound on yourself. As for the lion, at this moment he is even more upset than you are. Now that he has found you to be so untrustworthy and scatter-brained, he says that the wolf will be appointed king. Alas alack, what a wicked master he will be! What shall I do then? You are the one who has brought these evils upon all of us. But come, you must be more brave in the future and not let yourself be as easily frightened as some sheep from the flock. I swear these things to you by all the leaves on the trees and by every spring of water: I want to serve you and only you! There is nothing hostile about the lion's behaviour; his heartfelt wish is to make you queen of all the animals!' With these coaxing words, the fox persuaded the tawny deer to enter once again into that very abode of death. As soon as the lion had the deer trapped in the depths of his den, he enjoyed a full course meal, greedily devouring the flesh of the deer, drinking the marrow from her bones and feasting on her entrails. The fox, meanwhile, stood there waiting; after having delivered the deer, she was craving a share in the spoils. She stealthily grabbed the brains of the deer which had fallen to the ground and gobbled them up: this, then, was the booty which that sly boots got for her work. The lion, meanwhile, had made an inventory of all the deer's parts, and the brains were nowhere to be found. He searched around his couch and all over the house. Then the fox confounded the truth of the matter and said, 'That deer had no brains, so don't waste your time looking for them. What kind of brains do you expect from a creature who would come not once but twice into the den of a lion?'
ground_truth_moral: Never trust a known deceiver.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not let flattery or ambition blind you to a danger from which you have already narrowly escaped.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: He who disregards the lessons of a narrow escape to return to the same danger proves himself devoid of the wisdom necessary for survival.
summaries.proverb: Fool me once, shame on you; fool me twice, shame on me.
summaries.cot_proverb: He who falls twice into the same trap has no right to complain of his fate.

fable_text: There was a lion who strove to emulate the best sort of life lived in human society. He made his home inside a spacious den and tried to show genuine kindness to all those whom he recognized as the elite beasts of the mountain. His den often hosted a large crowd of such animals, who gathered together in a company and treated one another with civility. The lion would befriend and entertain them according to the rules of hospitality, placing before each of his guests their favourite dish, made with ingredients that the lion knew would give them pleasure. There was a fox who lived with the lion as his friend and companion, and the two of them were very happy together. Meanwhile, an elderly monkey served as the carver at the feasts, distributing the portions of meat to the lion's guests at dinner. Whenever there was a guest who was not one of the regular party, the monkey would set before him the same portion which he offered his master, namely, the quarry that the lion had seized in his latest hunting expedition. On these occasions, the fox received leftovers from the day before, and less than her usual portion. One day the lion happened to notice that the fox was pointedly refusing to speak and that she refrained from the meat served at dinner. The lion asked her what was the matter. 'My wise fox,' said the lion, 'speak to me as you used to do! Cheer up and take part in the banquet, my dear.' But the fox said, 'O lion, best among all the species of beasts, I am sick at heart and deeply worried. It is not only the present situation that distresses me; I am also saddened by things which I see are yet to come. If some new guest arrives with every passing day, one after the other, this will become a matter of custom and soon I will not even have leftover meat for my dinner.'
ground_truth_moral: Do not neglect the loyalty of old friends to please new acquaintances.

summaries.direct_moral: New acquaintances should not be favored at the expense of old and loyal companions.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of social prestige often leads to the neglect and displacement of one's most loyal companions in favor of new acquaintances.
summaries.proverb: Never sacrifice an old friend for a new acquaintance.
summaries.cot_proverb: Forsake not an old friend for a new one.

fable_text: At one time a very large and strong Wolf was born among the wolves, who exceeded all his fellow-wolves in strength, size, and swiftness, so that they unanimously decided to call him 'Lion.' The Wolf, with a lack of sense proportioned to his enormous size, thought that they gave him this name in earnest, and, leaving his own race, consorted exclusively with the lions. An old sly Fox, seeing this, said, 'May I never make myself so ridiculous as you do in your pride and self-conceit; for even though you have the size of a lion among wolves, in a herd of lions you are definitely a wolf.'
ground_truth_moral: Know yourself and your limits.

summaries.direct_moral: Relative superiority does not change one's fundamental nature.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Relative superiority does not alter one's fundamental nature.
summaries.proverb: Better to be the head of a dog than the tail of a lion.
summaries.cot_proverb: A big fish in a small pond is still just a fish.

fable_text: A fox was standing not far from a trap, pondering every possible approach. A nearby wolf noticed the fox and approached her. He then asked the fox if he might be allowed to take the meat, and the fox replied, 'Go ahead and help yourself! You're welcome to it, seeing as you are one of my very dearest friends.' The wolf rushed up without a moment's hesitation and as he learned forward over the trap he released the trigger so that the rod sprang free, striking his snout and forehead. 'If this is the sort of gift you give you to your friends,' said the wolf, 'why would anyone ever want to be your friend?'
ground_truth_moral: Flatterers and deceivers are not to be trusted.

summaries.direct_moral: Beware of gifts from those who seek to profit from your peril.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Beware the generosity of those who offer what they are afraid to take for themselves.
summaries.proverb: Beware of Greeks bearing gifts.
summaries.cot_proverb: Beware of a false friend’s generosity.

fable_text: A lamp, soaked with too much oil and flaring brightly, boasted that it gave more light than the sun. Then a sudden puff of wind arose, and the Lamp was immediately extinguished. Its owner lit it again, and said: 'Boast no more, but henceforth be content to give thy light in silence. Know that not even the stars need to be relit'
ground_truth_moral: Do not boast lest you be taken down.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not boast of a greatness that is easily extinguished.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Pride based on fleeting success is easily humbled by the inherent fragility of its source.
summaries.proverb: Pride goeth before a fall.
summaries.cot_proverb: Pride goes before a fall.

fable_text: The Wolves once selected one of their number to be their ruler. The Wolf that was chosen was a plausible, smooth-spoken rascal, and on a very early day he addressed an assembly of the Wolves as follows: 'One thing,' he said, 'is of such vital importance, and will tend so much to our general welfare, that I cannot impress it too strongly upon your attention. Nothing cherishes true brotherly feeling and promotes the general good so much as the suppression of all selfishness. Let each one of you, then, share with any hungry brother who may be near whatever in hunting may fall to your lot.' 'Hear, hear!' cried an Ass, who listened to the speech; 'and of course you yourself will begin with the fat Sheep that you hid yesterday in a corner of your lair.'
ground_truth_moral: Trust but verify.

summaries.direct_moral: Practice what you preach.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True leaders must practice the virtues they demand from others.
summaries.proverb: Practice what you preach.
summaries.cot_proverb: Practice what you preach.

fable_text: A bull was bitten by a Mouse and, angered by the wound, tried to capture him. But the Mouse reached his hole in safety. Though the Bull dug into the walls with his horns, he tired before he could rout out the Mouse, and crouching down, went to sleep outside the hole. The Mouse peeped out, crept furtively up his flank, and again biting him, retreated to his hole. The Bull rising up, and not knowing what to do, was sadly perplexed. At which the Mouse said, 'The great do not always prevail. There are times when the small and lowly are the strongest to do mischief.'
ground_truth_moral: The great do not always prevail.

summaries.direct_moral: Size and power are no guarantee of victory over the small and persistent.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Size and strength are no guarantee of superiority over agility and strategy.
summaries.proverb: Never underestimate a small adversary.
summaries.cot_proverb: A small enemy is not to be despised.

fable_text: A wayfaring man, traveling in the desert, met a woman standing alone and terribly dejected. He inquired of her, 'Who art thou?' 'My name is Truth,' she replied. 'And for what cause,' he asked, 'have you left the city to dwell alone here in the wilderness?' She made answer, 'Among the people of old, lies were found among only a few, but now they have spread throughout all of human society!'
ground_truth_moral: Falsehood is with all men.

summaries.direct_moral: Pervasive dishonesty drives truth into exile.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: When falsehood becomes universal, truth is forced into exile.
summaries.proverb: Truth finds no home in a world of lies.
summaries.cot_proverb: In a world of lies, Truth is an exile.

fable_text: The Sheep one day complained to the Shepherd that while they were shorn of their fleece, and their young ones often taken and killed for food, they received nothing in return but the green herbage of the earth, which grew of itself, and cost him no pains to procure. 'On the other hand, your Dog,' said they, 'which gives no wool, and is of no use for food, is petted and fed with as good meat as his master.' 'Peace, bleating simpletons!' replied the Dog, who overheard them; 'were it not that I look after and watch you, and keep off Wolves and thieves, small good would be to you your herbage or anything else.'
ground_truth_moral: Consider all before you judge.

summaries.direct_moral: Value the protection that makes your life and prosperity possible.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Protection is the essential prerequisite for the enjoyment of all other benefits.
summaries.proverb: Security is the foundation of prosperity.
summaries.cot_proverb: Without security, all other gains are worthless.

fable_text: An ass climbed up to the roof of a building, and frisking about there, broke in the tiling. The owner went up after him and quickly drove him down, beating him severely with a thick wooden cudgel. The Ass said, 'Why, I saw the Monkey do this very thing yesterday, and you all laughed heartily, as if it afforded you very great amusement.'
ground_truth_moral: People that live by example need to look closely at the example they are following.

summaries.direct_moral: What is appropriate for one may be disastrous for another.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: What is appropriate or amusing in one may be ridiculous or disastrous in another.
summaries.proverb: What is play for one is ruin for another.
summaries.cot_proverb: What is appropriate for one is not always appropriate for another.

fable_text: The Tail of a Serpent once rebelled against the Head, and said that it was a great shame that one end of any animal should always have its way, and drag the other after it, whether it was willing or no. It was in vain that the Head urged that the Tail had neither brains nor eyes, and that it was in no way made to lead. Wearied by the Tail's importunity, the Head one day let him have his will. The Serpent now went backwards for a long time, quite gaily, until he came to the edge of a high cliff, over which both Head and Tail went flying, and came with a heavy thump on the shore beneath. The Head was never again troubled by the Tail with a word about leading.
ground_truth_moral: Know your place.

summaries.direct_moral: Disaster ensues when those without vision or reason attempt to lead.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Ruin follows when the unqualified assume leadership over the capable.
summaries.proverb: Blind ambition leads to a fall.
summaries.cot_proverb: He who is unfit to lead should be content to follow.

fable_text: A shepherd penning his sheep in the fold for the night was about to shut up a wolf with them, when his Dog perceiving the wolf said, 'Master, how can you expect the sheep to be safe if you admit a wolf into the fold?'
ground_truth_moral: Be watchful or all will be lost.

summaries.direct_moral: Safety is impossible when you admit an enemy into your midst.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True security is impossible when one harbors the source of danger within the place of protection.
summaries.proverb: Don't let the fox guard the henhouse.
summaries.cot_proverb: He who welcomes the wolf into the fold invites his own ruin.

fable_text: Zeus once ordered Prometheus to show mankind the two ways: one the way of freedom and the other the way of slavery. Prometheus made the way of freedom rough at the beginning, impassable and steep, with no water anywhere to drink, full of brambles, and beset with dangers on all sides at first. Eventually, however, it became a smooth plain, lined with paths and filled with groves of fruit trees and waterways. Thus the distressing experience ended in repose for those who breath the air of freedom. The way of slavery, however, started out as a smooth plain at the beginning, full of flowers, pleasant to look at and quite luxurious, but in the end it became impassable, steep and insurmountable on all sides.
ground_truth_moral: It is better to endure short-term struggles for long-term benefits than the other way around.

summaries.direct_moral: The path to freedom requires initial hardship for lasting peace, while the path of slavery offers early ease at the cost of eventual ruin.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True liberty requires enduring initial hardship to achieve lasting peace, while the easy path leads to ultimate ruin.
summaries.proverb: Bitter is the path to freedom, but its end is sweet.
summaries.cot_proverb: The roots of freedom are bitter, but the fruit is sweet.

fable_text: A Frog and a Mouse, who had long been rivals for the sovereignty of a certain marsh, and had many a skirmish and running fight together, agreed one day to settle the matter, once for all, by a fair and open combat. They met, and each, armed with the point of a bulrush for a spear, was ready, if need be, to fight to the death. The fight began in earnest, and there is no knowing how it might have ended, had not a Kite, seeing them from afar, pounced down and carried off both heroes in her talons.
ground_truth_moral: When fools fight others run away with the prize.

summaries.direct_moral: Internal strife makes rivals easy prey for a common enemy.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Internal strife makes rivals easy prey for a common enemy.
summaries.proverb: When rivals fight, the enemy profits.
summaries.cot_proverb: When rivals fight over trifles, they become easy prey for their common enemy.

fable_text: When a fisherman happened to see an octopus during the winter, he said, 'If I take off my clothes and jump in after him, I will freeze! But if I do not catch that octopus, I condemn my children to die of starvation!'
ground_truth_moral: There is no easy choice during desperate times.

summaries.direct_moral: Dire necessity often forces a choice between two equally perilous evils.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Necessity often forces a choice between personal safety and the survival of those one is bound to protect.
summaries.proverb: Between the devil and the deep blue sea.
summaries.cot_proverb: Between the devil and the deep blue sea.

fable_text: A certain man was sitting on the beach counting the waves as they broke against the shore. When he lost count of the waves he got angry and frustrated. A sly fox then approached the man and said, 'Good sir, what is the point of getting angry about the waves that have already gone by? You need to just put them out of your mind and pick up counting again where you left off!'
ground_truth_moral: Do not dwell on what has passed.

summaries.direct_moral: Focus on moving forward rather than dwelling on what is already lost.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Focus on the present rather than grieving over the unalterable past.
summaries.proverb: Don’t cry over spilled milk.
summaries.cot_proverb: Let bygones be bygones.

fable_text: The clay which Prometheus used when he fashioned man was not mixed with water but with tears. Therefore, one should not try to dispense entirely with tears, since they are inevitable.
ground_truth_moral: Sadness is a part of life.

summaries.direct_moral: Sorrow is an inherent and inescapable part of the human condition.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Sorrow is an intrinsic and inescapable part of the human condition.
summaries.proverb: Sorrow is the lot of man.
summaries.cot_proverb: Sorrow is the lot of humanity.

fable_text: An owl, in her wisdom, counseled the Birds that when the acorn first began to sprout, to pull it all up out of the ground and not allow it to grow. She said acorns would produce mistletoe, from which an irremediable poison, the bird-lime, would be extracted and by which they would be captured. The Owl next advised them to pluck up the seed of the flax, which men had sown, as it was a plant which boded no good to them. And, lastly, the Owl, seeing an archer approach, predicted that this man, being on foot, would contrive darts armed with feathers which would fly faster than the wings of the Birds themselves. The Birds gave no credence to these warning words, but considered the Owl to be beside herself and said that she was mad. But afterwards, finding her words were true, they wondered at her knowledge and deemed her to be the wisest of birds. Hence it is that when she appears they look to her as knowing all things, while she no longer gives them advice, but in solitude laments their past folly.
ground_truth_moral: Destroy the seed of evil, or it will grow up to your ruin.

summaries.direct_moral: Heed wise counsel early to prevent future ruin.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Foresight is often dismissed as madness until the predicted disaster arrives.
summaries.proverb: Prevention is better than cure.
summaries.cot_proverb: Destroy the seed of evil, or it will grow to be your ruin.

fable_text: There was a woman of Sybaris who broke a jar. The jar then got someone to testify as a witness, but the woman of Sybaris said, 'I swear by the Maiden, you would have been far wiser if you'd gone right away to get a bandage, instead of making a court-case out of it.'
ground_truth_moral: Action is more valuable than arguments.

summaries.direct_moral: Practical remedies are wiser than formal litigation.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: It is more beneficial to seek a practical remedy for a misfortune than to pursue formal retribution for it.
summaries.proverb: Seek a remedy, not a verdict.
summaries.cot_proverb: Better to seek a remedy than a witness.

fable_text: The animals were Orpheus' companions, and while they enjoyed and admired his music, they never attempted to imitate it. But a few of the dogs, those shameless and meddlesome creatures, set out to make some music of their own. They straightaway went off by themselves to practice, changing themselves into human form as they continued their musical pursuits. This is where lyre players come from, and even today they cannot completely overcome their inborn nature. As a result, they preserve the teachings of Orpheus, but only to a small degree; for the most part it is the canine music that endures.
ground_truth_moral: Nature reveals itself.

summaries.direct_moral: Inherent nature will always reveal itself, regardless of outward transformation.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Inherent nature persists despite outward transformation or imitation.
summaries.proverb: Nature will out.
summaries.cot_proverb: What is bred in the bone will come out in the flesh.

fable_text: In the wintertime, a Dog curled up in as small a space as possible on account of the cold, determined to make himself a house. However when the summer returned again, he lay asleep stretched at his full length and appeared to himself to be of a great size. Now he considered that it would be neither an easy nor a necessary work to make himself such a house as would accommodate him.
ground_truth_moral: It's easy to put off today what can be done tomorrow.

summaries.direct_moral: Prepare for future adversity while you are in a state of comfort.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Prosperity often breeds a false sense of security that leads to the neglect of preparations required for adversity.
summaries.proverb: Vows made in the storm are forgotten in the calm.
summaries.cot_proverb: Prosperity forgets the resolutions of adversity.

fable_text: At one time, it seemed to all the beasts that they should hold a meeting and assembly, as there were frequent complaints among them about one another. When opinions were exchanged back and forth, the hares are said to have addressed the assembly: they believed it fair that all beasts should have the same dignity and equal power, and that it should not be the case that some are much stronger while others are weaker; for in this way, the inferior would be subject to the superior for plunder and prey. The Lions made this reply: 'Your words, O Hares! are good; but they lack both claws and teeth such as we have.'
ground_truth_moral: Be prepared to defend your position.

summaries.direct_moral: Ideals of equality are meaningless without the power to enforce them.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Arguments for equality are ineffective without the power to enforce them.
summaries.proverb: Might makes right.
summaries.cot_proverb: Fine words carry little weight without the strength to back them.

fable_text: An omnivorous, gluttonous mouse was creeping through the house when he noticed an oyster with its mouth wide open, so he bit into the false flesh of the oyster's wet beard. Suddenly the door to the oyster's house slammed shut in a painful clasp. The mouse was enclosed in a prison with no hope of escape: he himself had closed the door to the tomb of his own death.
ground_truth_moral: Greed often overreaches itself.

summaries.direct_moral: Unrestrained greed leads to self-destruction.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Unrestrained greed leads to self-inflicted destruction.
summaries.proverb: Greed digs its own grave.
summaries.cot_proverb: Greed often leads to its own destruction.

fable_text: Aesop said that the eyes were distressed because they considered themselves the most worthy part of the body, yet they saw the mouth enjoying every possible pleasure, especially honey, the sweetest substance of all. Filled with resentment, the eyes complained to the man. But when the man put honey in his eyes, they felt a stinging pain and burst into tears, since they thought that the honey was harsh and unpleasant.
ground_truth_moral: Be careful what you wish for.

summaries.direct_moral: What brings pleasure to one may bring pain to another.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: What is beneficial or pleasurable for one may be unsuitable and harmful for another.
summaries.proverb: One man’s meat is another man’s poison.
summaries.cot_proverb: One man's meat is another man's poison.

fable_text: Aesop, that wise man from Phrygia, once saw a winning athlete who was making extravagant boasts, so he asked whether the man's opponent had been more powerfully built. The athlete replied, 'Watch what you're saying! I was much stronger than he was.' 'You fool!' said Aesop. 'What kind of prize do you deserve if you were the stronger athlete and simply triumphed over an inferior opponent? You might have earned my grudging admiration if your opponent had in fact been superior to you in strength, so that you had to defeat him by means of your exceptional athletic prowess.'
ground_truth_moral: Degrading your opponents only diminishes your own victory.

summaries.direct_moral: Victory over an inferior opponent yields no true honor.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The merit of an achievement is measured by the difficulty of the challenge overcome.
summaries.proverb: There is no glory in an easy victory.
summaries.cot_proverb: There is no glory in an easy victory.

fable_text: A troublesome crow seated herself on the back of a Sheep. The Sheep, much against his will, carried her backward and forward for a long time, and at last said, 'If you had treated a dog in this way, you would have had your deserts from his sharp teeth.' To this the Crow replied, 'I despise the weak and yield to the strong. I know whom I may bully and whom I must flatter; and I thus prolong my life to a good old age.'
ground_truth_moral: Pick your enemies.

summaries.direct_moral: The wicked exploit the defenseless but yield to the powerful.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The unscrupulous exploit the defenseless while showing deference to those who can strike back.
summaries.proverb: Cowards are bold only against the defenseless.
summaries.cot_proverb: The wicked respect only those who can harm them.

fable_text: There is a bird that country folk call 'ground-bird,' which makes sense, because this bird makes her nest on the ground. A ground-bird happened to run into a conniving vixen, and as soon as she noticed her, the ground-bird flew even higher on her wings. 'Greetings!' said the vixen, 'May I ask why you are running away? It's not as if there were not plenty of food for me here in the field -- grasshoppers, beetles, locusts in abundance -- so there's nothing for you to be afraid of. I am actually very fond of you because of your retiring manner and honest ways.' The singing bird replied, 'Your words are certainly pleasant to hear, but I am no equal to you on the ground. Here in the air, though, I can hold my own. Why don't you come on up with me? This is the place where I would trust you with my life!'
ground_truth_moral: Flatters are not to be trusted.

summaries.direct_moral: Never trust the flattery of a natural enemy while in a position of vulnerability.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Never abandon a position of security for the flattering words of a natural adversary.
summaries.proverb: Trust not the fair words of a known enemy.
summaries.cot_proverb: Never trust the honeyed words of a known enemy.

fable_text: A Boy sat weeping upon the side of a well. A Thief happening to come by just at the same time, asked him why he wept. The Boy, sighing and sobbing, showed a bit of cord, and said that a silver tankard had come off from it, and was now at the bottom of the well. The Thief pulled off his clothes and went down into the well, meaning to keep the tankard for himself. Having groped about for some time without finding it, he came up again, and found not only the Boy gone, but his 0wn clothes also, the dissembling rogue having made off with them.
ground_truth_moral: Every tale is not to be believed.

summaries.direct_moral: A deceiver is often undone by his own greed.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Greed blinds the treacherous to the deceptions of others.
summaries.proverb: The deceiver is often deceived.
summaries.cot_proverb: The trickster is often caught in his own trap.

fable_text: Carried from his fresh-water pond by a rushing stream, a fresh-water fish rushed headlong into the waters of the sea. The shameless creature scorned the scaly schools in the sea and boasted endlessly of his own illustrious origins. A salt-water fish could not suffer this refugee in his own ancestral waters and he spoke these pungent words, naturally salted with wit: 'Enough of your empty lies and pretentious talk; you yourself are the best evidence of their absurdity. If the two of us are captured and hauled in by the same dripping net, I will be able to prove to you just who is more highly regarded by the crowd of onlookers: you will see that I am bought by the connoisseur at a very high price indeed, while you will be sold to an undiscriminating commoner for a mere penny or two!'
ground_truth_moral: Value is what others think of you, not what you think of yourself.

summaries.direct_moral: True value is proven by merit and reality, not by hollow boasting.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True worth is measured by objective value and the estimation of others rather than by boastful claims of one's origins.
summaries.proverb: True worth is measured by merit, not by boasts.
summaries.cot_proverb: A thing is worth only what it will bring.

fable_text: A countryman who needed to cross a river tested it by sounding it up and down to find the best place to ford. In his testing, he made this observation: where the water flowed smoothly, he found it to be the deepest; conversely, where it was shallow, it was where the water made the most noise.
ground_truth_moral: There is danger in the silent enemy.

summaries.direct_moral: Still waters run deep.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Still waters run deep.
summaries.proverb: Still waters run deep.
summaries.cot_proverb: Still waters run deep.

fable_text: A Mouse that was raised in a Chest, living all her days on what the Mistress of the House stored within, happened one day to tumble out over the edge and stumbled upon a very tasty morsel while searching for a way back inside. No sooner had she tasted it than she exclaimed how foolish she had been to believe that true happiness could only be found within that box.
ground_truth_moral: We should not love our homeland so much that we ignore the possibility of finding greater happiness elsewhere.

summaries.direct_moral: True perspective is found only by venturing beyond the limits of one's narrow experience.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Limited experience restricts one's understanding of the full scope of potential happiness.
summaries.proverb: Experience broadens a narrow horizon.
summaries.cot_proverb: The world is wider than your own four walls.

fable_text: A certain farmer complained that the beards of his corn sometimes cut the reapers' and threshers' fingers, and so he asked Ceres to make his corn grow without beards. His request was granted, and the little birds ate up all his grain. 'Fool that I was,' he said, 'to lose the support of my life rather than risk the pricking of my fingers.'
ground_truth_moral: A small inconvenience is compensated for by a greater usefulness.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not sacrifice essential protection to avoid minor discomfort.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Minor discomforts are often the necessary price of essential protection.
summaries.proverb: Better a little smart than a great loss.
summaries.cot_proverb: In avoiding a small evil, we often fall into a greater one.

fable_text: A Country Fellow had the luck to catch a Hawk chasing a Pigeon. The Hawk begged for her life, saying she never harmed the Country-man, so she hoped he wouldn't harm her. The Country-man replied, 'And what harm did the Pigeon ever do to you?' By your own reasoning, you should expect to be treated the same way you would have treated this Pigeon.
ground_truth_moral: It's wise to think before we speak.

summaries.direct_moral: Expect to be treated exactly as you treat others.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The standard of justice one applies to others is the standard by which one will be judged.
summaries.proverb: Do unto others as you would have them do unto you.
summaries.cot_proverb: Do unto others as you would have them do unto you.

fable_text: A Spider saw a Swallow catching Flies and quickly started making a Web to catch Swallows, thinking it was an invasion of her territory. But the Birds easily broke through the Web and flew away with it. Well, said the Spider, I see that catching Birds is not my skill; and so she went back to her usual work of catching Flies.
ground_truth_moral: A wise person will not attempt anything without having the right means to achieve it.

summaries.direct_moral: Stick to the work for which you are best suited.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: One should confine their ambitions to the scope of their own natural capabilities.
summaries.proverb: Stick to what you know.
summaries.cot_proverb: Know your own limitations.

fable_text: A Magpie asked a Pigeon why she kept nesting in the same place when her chicks were always taken before they could fly. 'That's my simplicity,' said the Pigeon. 'I mean no harm, and I suspect none.'
ground_truth_moral: Trusting as you wish to be trusted doesn't work between a rogue and an honest person.

summaries.direct_moral: Failing to learn from experience ensures the repetition of misfortune.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Innocence is no shield against the consequences of imprudence.
summaries.proverb: Innocence is no shield against misfortune.
summaries.cot_proverb: Innocence is no shield against misfortune.

fable_text: By the beak and claws of a Cuckoo, one might mistake her for a kind of Hawk, except one eats worms and the other eats meat. One day, a Hawk mocked a Cuckoo for her simple diet. 'If you want to look like a Hawk, why don't you live like one?' The Cuckoo felt a bit offended, but later, as she passed a pigeon house, she saw the skin of that very Hawk on a pole atop the dove house. 'Well!' said the Cuckoo, 'Wouldn't it have been better to eat worms than pigeons?'
ground_truth_moral: Pride and vanity leads to our downfall.

summaries.direct_moral: A humble, safe life is better than a dangerous, ambitious one.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: A humble and safe existence is preferable to a prestigious but perilous one.
summaries.proverb: Better a humble security than a perilous luxury.
summaries.cot_proverb: A humble security is better than a perilous grandeur.

fable_text: A fox was walking by a certain farm and saw a crowd of women who, in total silence, were eating many sumptuously roasted chickens. The fox turned to the women and said, 'What shouts there would be against me, and what howling of dogs, if I were to do what you are doing!' A certain old woman said, 'Most wretched creature! We are eating those things which are ours; you take your pleasure in things that belong to others.'
ground_truth_moral: We should not to conclude that we are allowed to do to other people's things what is permitted to them in their own houses.

summaries.direct_moral: What is lawful for the owner is a crime for the thief.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The morality of an action is determined by the actor's right to the resources involved.
summaries.proverb: Lawful possession justifies what theft condemns.
summaries.cot_proverb: One may do what one likes with one's own.

fable_text: There were many plump chickens together in a coop, some very fair and fat, while others did not grow well despite feeding. The fat ones would often make fun of the lean ones, calling them starvelings, until one day the cook was ordered to prepare some chickens for supper and to be sure to take the best from the pen. When that time came, those with the most flesh wished they had less, and it would have been better for them.
ground_truth_moral: The life of the poor is often safer than the life of the rich.

summaries.direct_moral: Prosperity can lead to ruin while humble circumstances offer safety.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Prominence often invites peril, while a humble state provides security.
summaries.proverb: Prosperity often brings its own peril.
summaries.cot_proverb: What you pride yourself on may be your undoing.

fable_text: The Tree was complaining about the Oxen's lack of gratitude. 'How often,' said the Tree, 'have I fed you with my leaves and sheltered you with my shade? And now you drag me over dirt and stones like this!' 'Alas!' cried the Oxen. 'Can't you see how we pant and groan, and how we are forced to do this?' The Tree realized they were doing it unwillingly and forgave them.
ground_truth_moral: What we are forced to do by a greater power and necessity is not truly our own action.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not blame those who are forced to act against their will.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Understanding the constraints and suffering of others transforms resentment into compassion.
summaries.proverb: Blame not those who act under compulsion.
summaries.cot_proverb: To understand all is to forgive all.

fable_text: There was a fine grove of trees, all tall, fair, and smooth, except for one small tree that was twisted and bent, and the others mocked it. The owner of the woods needed to build a house and told his workers to take timber from the grove, cutting down every tree that was suitable. They did as they were told, and this ugly little tree was left standing.
ground_truth_moral: Famous beauties are rarely lucky.

summaries.direct_moral: A perceived defect can be a life-saving advantage.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: What is perceived as a flaw may ultimately prove to be one's greatest protection.
summaries.proverb: Uselessness is a safeguard.
summaries.cot_proverb: A blessing in disguise.

fable_text: A Stork, who was there when a Swan was singing her final song, told her it was unusual to sing so much at this time. He asked her why she was doing it. The Swan replied, 'I am about to enter a place where I will no longer face traps, guns, or hunger. Who wouldn't be happy about such freedom?'
ground_truth_moral: Do not fear death, as it is the final goodbye to all the troubles, pains, and dangers of life.

summaries.direct_moral: Death is a welcome release from the hardships and dangers of life.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Death is a welcome release from the trials and tribulations of life.
summaries.proverb: Death is the release from all earthly suffering.
summaries.cot_proverb: Death is the ultimate liberation from life's hardships.

fable_text: A simple girl was wringing her hands and almost crying herself to death. And what was the matter? She had just parted ways with her sweetheart. 'Oh, you fool,' said one of her neighbors, 'to torment yourself over such a fellow!' 'No,' said the girl, 'I'm not so troubled about losing the man, but he took his coat with him. Honestly, when he gave me everything else he had in the world, I thought I might have that too, along with the rest.'
ground_truth_moral: Beware those who value their gain more than their love.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed is never satisfied, even by the most complete generosity.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Greed is insatiable and often values material gain more than human relationships.
summaries.proverb: Greed knows no limits.
summaries.cot_proverb: Greed knows no bounds.

fable_text: What a dust I raise! said the Fly on the Coach Wheel. And what a speed I drive at, said the same Fly again on the Horse's Back.
ground_truth_moral: Arrogant fools have no true influence.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not mistake your presence for your importance, nor claim credit for achievements that are not your own.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The insignificant often mistake their presence for the cause of great achievements.
summaries.proverb: Vanity claims credit for the labor of others.
summaries.cot_proverb: Vanity often claims credit for the work of others.

fable_text: You and I are so alike, says the Eel to the Snake, that I think we should be somewhat related; and yet those who hunt me are afraid of you. What could be the reason for this? Oh, says the Snake, because nobody harms me without paying for it.
ground_truth_moral: In all disputes, those who keep their opponents afraid of revenge come out best.

summaries.direct_moral: The power to retaliate deters harm and commands respect.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The ability to retaliate is the most effective deterrent against aggression.
summaries.proverb: Retaliation deters aggression.
summaries.cot_proverb: The power to strike back is the best defense.

fable_text: A donkey was complaining that he lacked horns, while a monkey was complaining that she lacked a tail. The mole said to them, 'Silence your complaining, since you can see that I am deprived of eyes.'
ground_truth_moral: Men would bear their misfortunes more patiently if they considered the ones of others.

summaries.direct_moral: Be grateful for what you have, as others endure far greater hardships.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Be content with your lot, for there are always others who suffer greater misfortunes.
summaries.proverb: Count your blessings; others are worse off.
summaries.cot_proverb: Count your blessings, for there are always others worse off than yourself.

fable_text: Once, a terrible storm blew at sea, and one sailor noticed that the rest of his companions were each praying to different saints. 'Be careful, my friends,' he said, 'what if we all drown before the messenger can deliver our message? Wouldn't it be better to pray directly to the one who can save us without any help?' With that, they turned their prayers to God himself, and the wind soon calmed.
ground_truth_moral: The shortest and surest way of doing things is best.

summaries.direct_moral: Seek help directly from the source rather than through intermediaries.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: In times of crisis, it is most effective to appeal directly to the ultimate authority rather than through intermediaries.
summaries.proverb: Go straight to the source.
summaries.cot_proverb: Go straight to the source.

fable_text: A Cook was frying a Dish of Live Fish, and as soon as they felt the Heat of the Pan, one cried out, 'We can't stand this!' and they all jumped into the Fire. Instead of making things better, they were worse off than before.
ground_truth_moral: When we are avoiding present dangers, we should not fall into even worse peril.

summaries.direct_moral: Attempting to escape one misfortune often leads to a greater one.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Impulsive attempts to escape a bad situation often lead to an even worse fate.
summaries.proverb: Out of the frying pan into the fire.
summaries.cot_proverb: Out of the frying pan into the fire.

fable_text: The Beasts formed an alliance with the Fishes against the Birds. War was declared; but the Fishes, instead of sending their share, sent their excuse, saying they could not travel by land.
ground_truth_moral: An alliance with the powerless is futile.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not rely on allies whose nature or circumstances prevent them from assisting you.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: An alliance with those whose nature prevents them from sharing the risk is worthless.
summaries.proverb: Never rely on an ally whose nature prevents their aid.
summaries.cot_proverb: Do not trust an alliance with those who cannot share your risks.

fable_text: An Ambassador, constantly bothered by drums and trumpets during his journey, wanted to save money. So, he kept telling them that he was in deep mourning for his mother and not in the mood for music. The drums and trumpets were as upset by this news as the Ambassador himself. A nobleman heard about this and paid a visit to express his condolences. 'My Lord,' asked the nobleman, 'how long has your mother been dead?' 'Oh,' replied the Ambassador, 'it's been about forty years now.' This explained the situation and ended the matter.
ground_truth_moral: There is a clever way of joking between seriousness and jest that brings both enjoyment and benefit.

summaries.direct_moral: Miserliness often resorts to transparent and absurd excuses.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Petty selfishness disguised as noble grief is eventually exposed by its own absurdity.
summaries.proverb: A liar should have a good memory.
summaries.cot_proverb: Parsimony is the parent of many a ridiculous lie.

fable_text: A clever gentleman, upon hearing that an old friend of his had been promoted to Cardinal, went to congratulate him on his new honor. 'Please, sir,' said the Cardinal, looking at him oddly, 'may I have your name and know your business?' 'I have come,' said the gentleman, 'to express my sympathy and to tell you how much I pity those burdened with high rank and position; for it affects their minds so much that they can no longer see, hear, or understand like other men, and makes them completely forget their old friends, as if they had never met them before.'
ground_truth_moral: Honors change behavior.

summaries.direct_moral: High rank often breeds a pride that makes men forget their old friends.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Sudden elevation in rank often breeds a vanity that leads individuals to forget their past and discard their old friends.
summaries.proverb: Honors change manners.
summaries.cot_proverb: Honors change manners.

fable_text: A mocking young rascal happened to meet an old man, whose age and weakness had bent his body like a bow. 'Please, Father,' he said, 'will you sell your bow?' 'Save your money, you fool,' replied the old man, 'for when you reach my age, you'll have such a bow for free.'
ground_truth_moral: Do not mock the feelings of old age, as no one can escape them if they go on living.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not mock in others the infirmities that time will eventually bring to you.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Never mock the infirmities of age, for time will eventually bestow them upon you.
summaries.proverb: Mock not what you will one day become.
summaries.cot_proverb: Mock not the infirmities of age, for they will one day be your own.

fable_text: There was a serious old man who lived to about seventy years without ever knowing the difference between a woman and a weather vane. The devil owed him a trick and repaid him by making the old fool marry a young girl. He often complained afterward about how poorly he had spent his time. 'When I was a young man,' he said, 'I wanted a wife, and now that I'm an old man, my wife wants a husband.'
ground_truth_moral: All things are to be done in their own time.

summaries.direct_moral: A desire long deferred becomes a burden when finally fulfilled.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The value of an opportunity is contingent upon the timeliness of its arrival relative to one's capacity to fulfill its demands.
summaries.proverb: Timing is everything.
summaries.cot_proverb: To everything there is a season.

fable_text: There was a lively and confident Magpie who thought the birds would never be well led until he himself was in charge. With this idea, he asked the Eagle to make him part of his council. 'I have the right look for it,' he said. 'I can talk and move quickly, and I can make as much noise and fuss as anyone with wings.' He was about to continue listing his skills when the Eagle kindly interrupted him. 'I know how well you can talk and how lively you are,' said the Eagle, 'but you chatter so much that people might think I've chosen a clown for a Prime Minister.'
ground_truth_moral: Big talkers are not suited for trust, business, or conversation.

summaries.direct_moral: Excessive talkativeness is often a sign of folly rather than a qualification for leadership.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Volubility and superficial activity are no substitutes for the wisdom and dignity required for leadership.
summaries.proverb: Empty vessels make the most noise.
summaries.cot_proverb: Empty vessels make the most noise.

fable_text: It's common when a strange Dog passes through a Town for a hundred Curs to bark at his tail, and every little Dog tries to bite him. There was one Dog who, seeing he couldn't save himself by running, turned on his Chasers, and found that one set of Teeth was better than two fast Legs; for when he did that, they all backed off and slunk away. A Captain used this example for his Soldiers. Fellow-Soldiers, he said, remember this: Those who run away are in more danger than those who stand and face the fight.
ground_truth_moral: Putting up with one insult invites another.

summaries.direct_moral: Facing a threat is often safer than fleeing from it.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Facing danger with courage is often safer than attempting to flee from it in fear.
summaries.proverb: He who flees invites pursuit.
summaries.cot_proverb: He who flies from danger is soonest overtaken.

fable_text: Whenever through their own fault something bad happens to them, people in general put the blame either on luck or on the devil; in fact, everybody acts this way on their own behalf in order to shed the blame. The devil doesn't like this! It happened that he noticed an old woman climbing a tree, he could anticipate that she was going to fall out of the tree and that she would put the blame on him. So he summoned witnesses and said: 'Look at that old woman climbing the tree, not at my recommendation; I can tell she is going to fall out of that tree. So you be my witnesses that I did not persuade her to climb up there in her shoes.' Soon encough the old woman fell down and when she was asked why she had climbed a tree wearing her shoes she said, 'The devil made me do it.' Then the devil called forward his witnesses and provided that this thing had been done by the old woman not at his recommendation.
ground_truth_moral: Only untrustworthy people blame the consequences of their own actions on others.

summaries.direct_moral: People often blame external forces for their own poor choices to avoid personal responsibility.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Humans often shift the blame for their own errors onto external forces to avoid personal accountability.
summaries.proverb: Blame not others for your own folly.
summaries.cot_proverb: We are quick to blame the devil for our own mistakes.

fable_text: As a group of Frogs were playing and jumping around in a Meadow, some Tortoises watching them felt upset that they couldn't do the same. But after a while, they noticed how these Frogs were caught and eaten by Birds and Fishes. One of the Tortoises said, 'It's better to live slow and steady than to die quick and lively.'
ground_truth_moral: Embrace your own strengths.

summaries.direct_moral: Security is better than risky excitement.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: A modest life of safety is preferable to a precarious life of excitement.
summaries.proverb: Safety is better than speed.
summaries.cot_proverb: Security is better than splendor.

fable_text: The Mice found it so troublesome to keep climbing the Oak for every bite they ate, that they decided to use their teeth to bring the Acorns down to them. But some were wiser than others, and an experienced old Mouse warned them to be careful. 'If we destroy our provider now, who will feed us later?' he said.
ground_truth_moral: Consider the consequences of your actions.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not destroy the source of your livelihood for immediate gain.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Do not destroy the source of your sustenance for the sake of temporary convenience.
summaries.proverb: Do not kill the goose that lays the golden eggs.
summaries.cot_proverb: Do not kill the goose that lays the golden eggs.

fable_text: There was a bob-tailed dog advertised in a newspaper, and someone who recognized him by his markings brought him back to his owner. The owner began to question the dog, asking how he could be so ungrateful and insensitive to run away from someone who was so kind to him. 'Did I ever hit you,' he asked, 'or even speak a harsh word to you in all the time you served me?' 'No,' replied the dog, 'not with your own hands or words, but you have had others do it for you many times. When I am beaten by your order, I consider it as if you yourself are beating me.'
ground_truth_moral: What a person does through another is truly their own action.

summaries.direct_moral: We are responsible for the actions we command others to perform.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Delegated cruelty remains the moral responsibility of the one who commands it.
summaries.proverb: He who commands the blow is the one who strikes.
summaries.cot_proverb: He who commands a wrong is as guilty as he who commits it.

fable_text: The Birds were once in a terrible fright, fearing gunshots from the Beetles. The reason was that the little balls of dirt the Beetles had gathered were mistaken by the Birds for bullets. But a Sparrow in the group, who was wiser than the others, told them to take heart. 'How can they reach us in the air,' he said, 'with those pellets they can barely roll on the ground?'
ground_truth_moral: Many people fear danger where there is none, and feel safe where there is danger: put things in perspective.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not fear a threat that lacks the means to harm you.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Reasoned assessment dispels the unfounded fears born of misinterpreted appearances.
summaries.proverb: Fear not a threat that cannot reach you.
summaries.cot_proverb: Reason dispels groundless fear.

fable_text: A Bear was once so angry at being stung by a Bee that he rushed into the Bee-Garden and knocked over all the Hives in revenge. This act brought out swarms of Bees against him, and he later realized it would have been wiser to ignore one sting than to provoke a thousand with his pointless anger.
ground_truth_moral: It's better to ignore an insult from one troublemaker than to bring a whole crowd of trouble upon yourself.

summaries.direct_moral: It is better to bear a single injury than to provoke a thousand by seeking revenge.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: It is better to endure a minor injury than to invite a greater calamity through impulsive revenge.
summaries.proverb: Anger is more harmful than the injury that provoked it.
summaries.cot_proverb: It is better to suffer a single injury in silence than to provoke a thousand by seeking revenge.

fable_text: A bird catcher who had set up his net and bait, sat in his spot to watch for a catch. Many birds came one after another, landing and pecking for a while before flying away again. They kept coming and going all day long, but so few at a time that the man didn't think it was worth catching them. Finally, after missing all his chances hoping for a bigger catch, evening came and the birds went to sleep. He had to either pull the net then or not at all, and in the end, he had to be satisfied with just one chaffinch that was unlucky enough to be out later than the others.
ground_truth_moral: Do not trade what might come, which is uncertain, for present opportunities, which can never be regained.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed for a greater gain often leads to the loss of smaller, certain opportunities.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Greed for a greater gain often results in the loss of certain, smaller opportunities.
summaries.proverb: A bird in the hand is worth two in the bush.
summaries.cot_proverb: A bird in the hand is worth two in the bush.

fable_text: A Soldier who already had one excellent horse bought another that was not nearly as good, yet he took more care of the new one than the first. Everyone wondered at this, since the new horse was neither as beautiful nor as useful as the other. 'Ah,' said someone, 'it's natural to be fond of the newest arrival.'
ground_truth_moral: Everything pleases at first, but nothing pleases for long.

summaries.direct_moral: Novelty is often prized more than proven merit.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The allure of novelty often leads to the neglect of proven merit.
summaries.proverb: Novelty often outweighs merit.
summaries.cot_proverb: Novelty is often preferred to merit.

fable_text: I wonder, said a Sow to a Spaniel, how you can be so loyal to a Master who gives you so many blows and pulls your ears. Well, said the Dog, but then consider the good food and kind words he gives me, and I'm better off for it.
ground_truth_moral: We should not be upset by the words of our teachers, since those words are usually the cause of many good things.

summaries.direct_moral: Gratitude for consistent benefits outweighs the memory of occasional severity.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Benefits received often outweigh the memory of injuries endured.
summaries.proverb: Take the bitter with the sweet.
summaries.cot_proverb: Gratitude for benefits outweighs the memory of injuries.

fable_text: Why don't you hurry up? cried the Timber in the Cart to the Oxen pulling it. The load isn't that heavy, surely. Well! said the Oxen, if you knew your own fate, you wouldn't be so cheerful about ours. We will be free of our load as soon as we reach our journey's end, but you, destined to be beams and supports, will have to bear weight until you break. This made the Timber understand the situation better.
ground_truth_moral: It's wise and kind to be considerate of one another, for no one knows their own end.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not mock the toil of others when a greater or more permanent burden awaits you.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Do not mock the temporary struggles of others when your own destiny holds a permanent burden.
summaries.proverb: Mock not the burden of others, for your own fate may be heavier.
summaries.cot_proverb: Do not mock the temporary toils of others when your own fate is a heavier burden.

fable_text: A Goldfinch escaped from his Master's Cage, and the Master tried to get him back, but the bird refused to return. 'Well!' said the Boy, 'you'll regret it, because you'll never be as well cared for anywhere else.' 'That may be true,' said the Bird, 'but I would rather take care of myself than be under your care.'
ground_truth_moral: There is no wealth greater than liberty.

summaries.direct_moral: Freedom is more valuable than the security of captivity.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Liberty is more valuable than any comfort provided by captivity.
summaries.proverb: Better a crust in liberty than a feast in chains.
summaries.cot_proverb: Better a crust with liberty than a feast in a cage.

fable_text: Practically all the birds were invited to the Eagle's wedding. They resented the fact that the Hoopoe had been given a higher seat of honor than the other birds because the Hoopoe was notable for its crown and for being adorned with multicolored feathers, although it was accustomed to spend all its time rolling around in dung and filth.
ground_truth_moral: True worth should be measured by character and deeds, not by outward appearances.

summaries.direct_moral: External beauty cannot hide a base or filthy character.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: External splendor frequently masks an underlying lack of true merit or character.
summaries.proverb: Fine feathers do not make fine birds.
summaries.cot_proverb: Fine feathers do not make fine birds.

fable_text: There was a certain gluttonous priest who had set out on a journey far from home to a wedding to which he had been invited. On the way he found a heap of pears, but he didn't touch so much as a single one of them, although he was feeling very hungry; rather, he made a mockery of them and sprinkled them with piss, for he resented that food of this sort was offered to him on his journey when he was on his way to a splendid wedding feast. But when along the way he ran into a certain river, it was so swollen with rain that he could not cross it without losing his life, and so he decided to go back home. On the way home, starving, he was oppressed by such great hunger that if he had not eaten those very pears, on which he had pissed, he would have died.
ground_truth_moral: Nothing should be condemned, since there is nothing so worthless and humble that it cannot at some time or another prove to be useful.

summaries.direct_moral: Never despise humble things, for you may later find yourself in desperate need of them.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Do not despise or ruin even the most humble resource, for you cannot foresee when your survival might depend upon it.
summaries.proverb: Never say, "Of this water I will not drink."
summaries.cot_proverb: Never say of any water, "I will not drink of it."

fable_text: A Hog noticed a Horse in the peak of his bravery, just about to charge at an Enemy. 'Why, what a fool you are,' said the Hog to him, 'to rush so quickly to your own destruction?' 'That thought,' said the Horse, 'might suit a wicked creature like you, who is only fattened up to be killed by a knife. But when I'm gone, I'll leave behind the memory of a good name.'
ground_truth_moral: It is more honorable to die having achieved noble deeds than to live a life of vice.

summaries.direct_moral: An honorable death is preferable to a base and purposeless life.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: An honorable death is superior to a base and purposeless existence.
summaries.proverb: Better a glorious death than a shameful life.
summaries.cot_proverb: Death with honor is better than life with ignominy.

fable_text: A Tanner bought a Bear-Skin from a Hunter and paid him cash for it. The Hunter told him he would kill a Bear the next day and the Tanner could have the Skin.
