Out of curiosity, the Tanner went with the Hunter to the Hunt and climbed a tree to watch. The Hunter bravely approached the Bear's Den and sent his Dogs in after it. The Bear rushed out immediately, and the Hunter missed his shot, so the Bear knocked him over. The Hunter held his breath and lay perfectly still, pretending to be dead. The Bear sniffed him, thought he was a corpse, and left him alone. Once the Bear was gone and the danger had passed, the Tanner came down from the tree and told the Hunter to get up. 'Listen, my Friend,' said the Tanner, 'the Bear whispered something in your ear. What was it, I ask?' 'Oh,' said the Hunter, 'he told me to be careful in the future and make sure to catch the Bear before selling its Skin.'
ground_truth_moral: Don't promise more than you can deliver.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not sell what you have not yet obtained.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: One should not dispose of the rewards of a venture before the victory is secured.
summaries.proverb: Don’t count your chickens before they hatch.
summaries.cot_proverb: Never sell the skin till you have caught the bear.

fable_text: There was a Holy Man who spoke to a Soldier about his line of work, pointing out the dangers, sins, and troubles that come with it. 'For the sake of your soul, sir, please give it up,' he said. 'Well, Father,' replied the Soldier, 'I'll do as you say; because we are paid so poorly, and there's so little to gain from plundering, I think I might as well turn to a godly life.'
ground_truth_moral: Many people renounce wicked activities only because they are prevented from conducting them any longer.

summaries.direct_moral: Virtue is often embraced only when vice ceases to be profitable.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Men often embrace virtue only when vice ceases to be profitable.
summaries.proverb: When vice ceases to pay, men turn to virtue.
summaries.cot_proverb: Many make a virtue of necessity.

fable_text: A certain man, after the death of his wife whom he had greatly loved, married another woman who was herself a widow. She continuously prattled to him about the virtues and great deeds of her late husband. The man, in order to give tit for tat, also talked all the time about the excellent character and remarkable good wisdom of his late wife. One day, however, the woman grew angry and gave to a poor man who was begging alms part of the chicken she had roasted for their dinner, telling the beggar: I give this to you on behalf of the soul of my previous husband. When her spouse heard this, he summoned the beggar and gave him the other half of the chicken, saying: I also give this to you, for the soul of my late wife. In this way, the two of them, as they wanted to injure one another, ended up not having anything to eat for dinner.
ground_truth_moral: Do not fight with those who are perfectly capable of fighting back.

summaries.direct_moral: In seeking to spite others, we often only bring harm upon ourselves.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Spiteful competition intended to wound another often results in one's own deprivation.
summaries.proverb: Cut off your nose to spite your face.
summaries.cot_proverb: In seeking to spite another, we often harm ourselves.

fable_text: A Lion found himself trapped in a Net and called to a Mouse passing by to help him out, promising never to forget the kindness. The Mouse gnawed the threads apart, and after freeing the Lion, asked in return for his Daughter's hand in marriage. The Lion, being generous, could not refuse. But unfortunately, as the new Bride was about to step into the Marriage Bed, she accidentally stepped on her Husband and crushed him to death.
ground_truth_moral: Unequal matches bring misery.

summaries.direct_moral: Unequal matches lead to ruin.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Mismatched alliances are doomed by the inherent incompatibility of the parties involved.
summaries.proverb: Unequal alliances lead to ruin.
summaries.cot_proverb: Unequal alliances lead to ruin.

fable_text: There was once a debate about Wax and Brick, questioning why Wax was so fragile and easily broken, while Brick could withstand all damage and weather, remaining strong and firm. The Wax thought about this and concluded that it was the burning that made the Brick hard. So, it threw itself into the fire, believing that heat would harden the Wax too. But what made the Brick solid, melted the Wax.
ground_truth_moral: It's foolish to make decisions without understanding the nature of the matter at hand.

summaries.direct_moral: What strengthens one may destroy another.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: What strengthens one nature may destroy another.
summaries.proverb: The same sun that melts the wax hardens the clay.
summaries.cot_proverb: What hardens the brick melts the wax.

fable_text: 'Oh, the endless misery of the life I lead!' cries the hard-working farmer, 'spending all my days plowing, sowing, digging, and fertilizing, and in the end, gaining nothing from it! Now, in a soldier's life, there's honor to be earned, and one lucky strike can set a man up forever. Indeed, I'll sell my stock, get a horse and arms, and try my luck in war'. Off he goes, makes his attempt, faces the battle, and ends up leaving a leg or an arm behind to return home. By this time, he's had enough of being a knight-errant, and a new idea strikes him. He thinks he might do better as a merchant. No sooner does this thought excite him than he gets a ship, loads it, and sets off to sea on an adventure: building castles in the air and imagining both the Indies in his coffers before he's even left the port. Well! And what's the end of all this? He encounters bad weather, among shoals and rocks, where merchant, vessel, goods, and all are lost in one common wreck.
ground_truth_moral: Everyone should be satisfied with their own lot in life, given that disaster awaits us on every side.

summaries.direct_moral: Discontent with one's station often leads to greater misfortune.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Discontentment with one's lot often leads to the pursuit of hazardous shortcuts that result in total ruin.
summaries.proverb: The grass is always greener on the other side.
summaries.cot_proverb: The grass is always greener on the other side.

fable_text: A donkey was frustrated that a certain buffoon was given a public office and was wrapped in elegant clothes because he was able to make loud noises come out of his gut. So the donkey went to the city officials, asking that they would honor him no less than they had done the buffoon. When the city officials, surprised at the donkey's request, asked him why he considered himself worthy of such honor, the donkey said: 'Because I emit even louder farts than the buffoon does, and what's more they don't stink.'
ground_truth_moral: When public officials encourage foolishness, it's no surprise if fools try to become public officials.

summaries.direct_moral: Excellence in a vulgar or trivial pursuit does not confer true merit or worthiness for honor.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True merit is not established by excelling in trivial or base endeavors.
summaries.proverb: To excel in folly is no cause for pride.
summaries.cot_proverb: To excel in what is shameful is no honor.

fable_text: There was a dispute between a River and a Fountain about which of the two should be preferred. The River boasted about the abundance and variety of fish it produced, the benefits of navigation, and the many grand towns and palaces built along its banks for the pleasure of the location. It claimed to provide great satisfaction to mankind in terms of both convenience and delight. Meanwhile, the Fountain (said the River) passed obscurely through the earth's caverns, lay buried in moss, and crept into the world as if ashamed to show its head. The Fountain was so offended by this arrogance and vanity that it stopped its spring and ceased the flow of its waters. As a result, the River's channel dried up, leaving the fish dead and rotting in the mud.
ground_truth_moral: He who claims any good for himself takes away from the source of all the good he enjoys.

summaries.direct_moral: Never despise the source of your own prosperity.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: One must never despise the humble source of their own success.
summaries.proverb: Never despise the source of your own success.
summaries.cot_proverb: Do not despise the source of your greatness.

fable_text: A notorious criminal, who had committed countless crimes and been through many jails, made a friend of the Devil to help him out of all his troubles. This friend helped him many times, but each time he was caught again, he turned to the Devil for help once more. But on his last call, the Devil came to him with a big bag of old shoes on his back and said plainly, 'Friend, I'm at the end of my line and can help you no longer. I have worn out all these shoes in your service and have not a penny left to buy more. So you must excuse me if I leave you here.'
ground_truth_moral: We should not expect that our crimes will always go unpunished.

summaries.direct_moral: Evil alliances provide no permanent escape from the consequences of one's actions.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: No amount of external aid can indefinitely shield a person from the inevitable consequences of their own persistent misdeeds.
summaries.proverb: Even the Devil’s luck eventually runs out.
summaries.cot_proverb: The Devil leaves his own in the lurch at last.

fable_text: he birds were deliberating about whether they should elect additional kings, since the eagle alone was not able to rule so many flocks of birds. They would have voted in favor, except that at the warning of the crow they stopped short of adopting the plan: for when the crow was asked for what reason she did not think that more kings ought to be elected, she said, 'Because it is more effort for many sacks to be filled than just one.'
ground_truth_moral: It is better by far to be governed by one chief, than by many.

summaries.direct_moral: Multiple rulers impose a greater burden on their subjects than a single leader.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Multiplying the number of rulers only increases the burden of their maintenance upon the governed.
summaries.proverb: Many masters, many burdens.
summaries.cot_proverb: Better one master than many.

fable_text: A Poor Woman was nearly out of her mind with fear of losing her Husband. The Good Man was Sick and given up on, and nothing would do but that Death must take her instead of him. She called and prayed, and prayed and called, until at last Death appeared in a terrible form at her side. She politely dropped him a curtsy: 'And please, Sir,' she said, 'do not mistake yourself; for the person you came for is lying in the bed there.'
ground_truth_moral: No one is so loving of a friend that they would not prefer their own good to that of another.

summaries.direct_moral: People often lack the courage to fulfill the sacrifices they so loudly proclaim.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The instinct for self-preservation often outweighs even the most passionate declarations of self-sacrifice.
summaries.proverb: Self-preservation is the first law of nature.
summaries.cot_proverb: Self-preservation is the first law of nature.

fable_text: There was a good man who followed his wife's body to the grave, weeping and wailing all the way, while his son followed the coffin singing. 'Why, you rascal,' said the father, 'you should howl and wring your hands and do as I do, not go singing like a madman.' 'But father,' said the son, 'you pay the priests to sing, so why be angry with me for giving you a song for free?' 'Well,' said the father, 'what suits the priests doesn't always suit you. It's their job to sing, but it's your part to cry.'
ground_truth_moral: Not all things are suitable for all people.

summaries.direct_moral: Observe the behavior appropriate to your station and the occasion.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Propriety is determined by the specific obligations of one’s social role rather than the actions of others.
summaries.proverb: Let every man act his part.
summaries.cot_proverb: To every man his proper part.

fable_text: A certain man, German by birth, and exceedingly rich, was ailing. A large number of doctors had come to cure him (flies do indeed come swarming in crowds to honey), and one of them said among other things that the man needed an enema if he wanted to get well. When the man, who was unfamiliar with this medical procedure, heard what the doctor said, he flew into a rage and ordered that the doctors be thrown out of his house, saying that they must be insane since they wanted to cure his butt when it was his head that hurt.
ground_truth_moral: Even beneficial things all appear to be drastic and sure to hurt for those those who are unfamiliar with them and inexperienced.

summaries.direct_moral: Ignorance leads people to reject necessary remedies simply because they do not understand them.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Ignorance often mistakes wisdom for madness when it cannot perceive the indirect path to a solution.
summaries.proverb: Ignorance rejects the remedy it does not understand.
summaries.cot_proverb: He who will not be counseled cannot be helped.

fable_text: There was a donkey who was ill, and the story went out that he was soon going to die, so when the wolves and the dogs came to visit him and asked his son how his father was feeling, the son replied through a crack in the door: 'Better than you wish.'
ground_truth_moral: Many pretend to be concerned about some people's demise, when however what they want is for it to come quickly.

summaries.direct_moral: Beware the false concern of those who stand to profit from your misfortune.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Beware the false sympathy of those who profit from your misfortune.
summaries.proverb: The predator’s disappointment is the prey’s health.
summaries.cot_proverb: The concern of the wicked is but a mask for their own interest.

fable_text: A donkey was suffering terribly during the winter time because he was affected by the extreme cold and he had tough chaff fodder. Therefore, he was hoping for the moderation of spring and its tender grasses. But when spring came, he was compelled by his master (who was a potter) to carry the clay into the work area, to carry wood for the oven, and from there to carry the bricks, tiles and roof-tiles to various places. Completely tired of spring, in which season he had to endure so much hard work, he was asking in all his prayers for summer to come, so that his master would be busy with the harvest and they would allow him to rest. But then too, when he was compelled to carry the harvest to the threshing-floor and from there to carry the wheat into the house, and there was not place of rest for him, the donkey was hoping that at least autumn would be an end of his labors. But not even at that time did the donkey see an end of his troubles, since every day there was wood, apples and wine to be carried, so again he begged for the snow and ice of winter, so that at least there would be some rest allotted to him from so much hard work.
ground_truth_moral: Every Season has its own work.

summaries.direct_moral: Be content with the present, for every change of circumstances brings its own burdens.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: A change of circumstances often merely replaces one set of hardships with another.
summaries.proverb: Be content with your lot.
summaries.cot_proverb: He who is discontented with his lot will find no relief in change.

fable_text: As a group of mice were peeking out of their holes to see what was around, they spotted a cat on a shelf. The cat looked so calm and harmless, as if she had no life or spirit in her. 'Well,' said one of the mice, 'that's a kind creature, I'm sure of it. You can see it in her face, and I really want to get to know her.' No sooner said than done; but as soon as the cat had the mouse within reach, she torn her to pieces.
ground_truth_moral: People are to be judged by their actions, not by their appearance.

summaries.direct_moral: Appearances are often deceptive.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Appearances are deceptive, and a predator’s stillness should not be mistaken for a change in its nature.
summaries.proverb: Appearances are deceptive.
summaries.cot_proverb: Appearances are deceptive.

fable_text: The Porcupine and the Hedgehog are quite similar, but the Porcupine has longer and sharper quills, which he can shoot at an enemy. A Wolf wanted to deal with him, but first needed to disarm him. So, the Wolf told the Porcupine in a friendly manner that it didn't look good to be armed during peaceful times, as if they were at war. He advised the Porcupine to put his quills aside, saying he could pick them up whenever he wanted. The Porcupine replied, 'You talk of peace? Well, my situation is one of war, and that's why I keep my quills ready as long as a Wolf is around.'
ground_truth_moral: No one can be safe in peace unless they are always ready to face an enemy in case of war.

summaries.direct_moral: Never abandon your defenses in the presence of a known threat.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Never abandon your defenses as long as a known threat remains present.
summaries.proverb: In times of peace, prepare for war.
summaries.cot_proverb: Never trust the advice of a known enemy.

fable_text: A Simple Mouse happened to be nearby when a Kite got caught in a Net. The Kite begged the Mouse to help her get free. The Mouse gnawed a Hole in the Net and set the Kite free; and the Kite ate the Mouse for her trouble.
ground_truth_moral: Save a Thief from the Gallows, and he'll cut your Throat.

summaries.direct_moral: Kindness shown to the wicked is often repaid with treachery.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Kindness shown to the wicked is often repaid with injury.
summaries.proverb: No good deed goes unpunished.
summaries.cot_proverb: Favors to the wicked are repaid with injury.

fable_text: In old times, when Jupiter was in the mood to grant requests, a Cockle asked that his house and body be all one piece. Jupiter replied that it would be a burden rather than a favor. 'Yes', said the Cockle, 'but it is a burden I would rather bear than live exposed to bad neighbors.'
ground_truth_moral: Unwanted visits are a nuisance to a peaceful person's life.

summaries.direct_moral: Security is worth the sacrifice of convenience.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The burden of self-protection is a price worth paying for security from malicious neighbors.
summaries.proverb: Better a heavy house than a bad neighbor.
summaries.cot_proverb: Better a heavy house than a bad neighbor.

fable_text: A Snake was convinced during a Cold Winter to let a Hedgehog into his Den. But once inside, the Hedgehog's spines were very uncomfortable for the Snake. So the Snake told him he must find another place, as the Den was too small for both of them. The Hedgehog replied, 'If you can't stay, you should leave. As for me, I'm quite happy here, and if you're not, you're free to go.'
ground_truth_moral: Possession is nine-tenths of the law.

summaries.direct_moral: Think twice before admitting a guest who may prove impossible to remove.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Be cautious whom you admit to your home, for an ungrateful guest may eventually drive out the host.
summaries.proverb: Give an inch and they'll take a mile.
summaries.cot_proverb: Be careful whom you harbor, lest they drive you from your own home.

fable_text: A Fox and a Hare were once in a heated contest about which of them could manage best in the world. 'When I am chased,' said the Hare, 'I can show the dogs a clean pair of heels and run away from them easily.' 'And yet,' said the Fox, 'I have outsmarted more of them with my tricks and cunning than you ever did with your speed.'
ground_truth_moral: Wisdom is far superior to strength.

summaries.direct_moral: Different talents are equally effective in overcoming life's challenges.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Success may be attained through either physical prowess or intellectual cunning.
summaries.proverb: Wit is better than speed.
summaries.cot_proverb: Wit is better than speed.

fable_text: There was an old man, who, even in his frailty, still had thoughts of women. His spiritual advisor noticed this and gave him earnest advice about the desires of the flesh. The old man thanked him sincerely for his kind and Christian counsel, saying, 'With Heaven's help, I'll follow it. To be honest, I'm told it's not good for me, and truly, my body is no longer fit for such activities.'
ground_truth_moral: Many people give up their vices not out of a love for virtue, but because they can no longer continue indulging in them.

summaries.direct_moral: Virtue is easily claimed when one lacks the capacity for vice.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Renouncing a vice one can no longer commit is not virtue, but necessity.
summaries.proverb: Make a virtue of necessity.
summaries.cot_proverb: We make a virtue of necessity.

fable_text: A certain impertinent, thoughtless fellow decided to visit a philosopher. He found him alone in his study and wondered how he could bear to live such a solitary life. The learned man told him, 'Sir, you are very mistaken; I was in very good company until you came in.'
ground_truth_moral: Better to be alone than in bad company.

summaries.direct_moral: A wise person is never less alone than when alone with their thoughts.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: A rich inner life provides a companionship that makes solitude preferable to the society of the shallow.
summaries.proverb: Never less alone than when alone.
summaries.cot_proverb: A wise man is never less alone than when alone.

fable_text: There is a story of a Wolf that wrapped himself in a sheep's skin and attacked lambs for a while under that disguise. But the shepherd eventually caught him and hung him up, sheep's skin and all, on a high gallows as a warning and example. The neighbors were amazed and asked why he hung up his sheep. 'Oh,' he said, 'that's just the skin of a sheep that was used to cover the heart, malice, and body of a wolf that hid under it.'
ground_truth_moral: People should not be judged by their appearances, but by their actions.

summaries.direct_moral: Deception eventually leads to one's own destruction.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Deception cannot permanently conceal one's true nature or shield one from the consequences of their actions.
summaries.proverb: Appearances are deceptive.
summaries.cot_proverb: Appearances are deceptive.

fable_text: There was a Widow who had a desire for a Second Husband, and she asked a gossiping Friend of hers for help on how to handle it. 'The truth is,' she said, 'I really want another partner, but people would laugh and point at me, and it would make me miserable.' 'You're a fine Widow,' said the Friend, 'to worry about what people say. Listen to me now. You have a Donkey in your fields; go and have that Donkey painted Green, and then let him be shown around the Country. Do this without any more talk, because talk just wastes time.' So it was done, and for the first four or five days, the Green Donkey had the whole Country following him, men, women, and children, all staring and shouting. In four or five more days, the excitement was over, and the Donkey could travel all day without anyone noticing him. 'Now,' said the helpful Friend, 'a newly married Widow is like a Green Donkey: everyone talks about it for the first few days, and then the story fades away.'
ground_truth_moral: There is no thing so worthy of wonderment which does not cease to be a miracle with the passage of time.

summaries.direct_moral: Public scrutiny is fleeting and gossip quickly fades as novelty wears off.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Public scrutiny is a fleeting sensation that inevitably gives way to indifference.
summaries.proverb: A nine days' wonder is soon forgotten.
summaries.cot_proverb: A wonder lasts but nine days.

fable_text: A Shepherd had one favorite dog that he trusted more than all the others. He fed him by hand and cared for him more than any of the other dogs. This kindness continued for a long time until, when some sheep went missing, he suspected this dog of being unfaithful. After this suspicion, he watched him closely and eventually discovered that this trusted servant was the thief. Once he found out, he had the dog caught and told him to prepare for punishment. 'Oh, Master,' said the dog, 'I am part of your family, and it would be harsh to punish a household member. Turn your anger on the wolves instead, who regularly attack your sheep.' 'No, no,' said the Shepherd, 'I would rather spare forty wolves who make it their business to kill sheep than one sheep-stealing dog trusted with their care. There is some honesty and boldness in the wolves, but the dog's betrayal is the worst kind of treachery.'
ground_truth_moral: An enemy in disguise is much more unforgivable than an open enemy.

summaries.direct_moral: Betrayal by a trusted friend is more heinous than the open hostility of a known enemy.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Betrayal by a trusted ally is a more grievous offense than the predictable hostility of a known enemy.
summaries.proverb: A false friend is more dangerous than an open enemy.
summaries.cot_proverb: A false friend is more dangerous than an open enemy.

fable_text: There was once a Ram who defeated all his fellow rams and became so proud of his victories that he decided to challenge a Bull to a fight. They met, and at the first clash, the Ram was knocked out and lay as if dead. When he came to, he said, 'This is the result of my arrogance and foolishness in challenging an enemy that nature made stronger than me.'
ground_truth_moral: We should not pick fights with people stronger than we are.

summaries.direct_moral: Know your limits and do not let pride lead you to challenge a superior force.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Pride born of success among equals often leads to the ruinous folly of challenging the naturally superior.
summaries.proverb: Pride goeth before a fall.
summaries.cot_proverb: Pride goes before a fall.

fable_text: A certain father, whose son had succumbed to vicious tendencies, was urging the boy at great length that he should quit the way of vices and keep his eye instead on the virtues, which would engender praise and honor for him. The son replied: 'You are wasting your time, Father, in urging me to do these things, for I have heard many a preacher, so to speak, and they urged me to the path of virtue with far more talent than you, but I have still never taken any of their advice!'
ground_truth_moral: Men who are wickedly inclined will not stop their vicious behavior, no matter who urges them to do so.

summaries.direct_moral: Persuasion is useless to those who are determined to remain in vice.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Eloquent persuasion is powerless against a heart committed to vice.
summaries.proverb: He who will not be counseled cannot be helped.
summaries.cot_proverb: He who will not be counseled cannot be helped.

fable_text: There was an Eagle that took a nest of Rabbits and carried them away to her young. The Mother Rabbit followed her with tears in her eyes, pleading in the name of all the powers that protect the innocent and oppressed, to have mercy on her poor children. But the Eagle, in a fit of pride and anger, tore them to pieces. The Rabbit then gathered all the rabbits together, told her story, and sought advice on revenge. 'Divine justice,' she said, 'will not let such cruel acts go unpunished.' They discussed the matter and all agreed that the only way to repay the Eagle was to undermine the tree where she nested. So they all worked at the roots of the tree, leaving it with so little support that the first strong wind brought it crashing down, nest, Eagles, and all. Some of them were killed by the fall; others were eaten by birds and beasts of prey, and the Rabbit finally had the satisfaction of destroying the Eagle's children in revenge for her own.
ground_truth_moral: It is very unwise, even for the greatest of men, to provoke the least, as they may find a way to enact revenge.

summaries.direct_moral: Arrogant cruelty inevitably invites its own destruction at the hands of the oppressed.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The powerful should never underestimate the capacity of the weak to seek retribution for injustice.
summaries.proverb: No one is so powerful that the weak cannot bring them to ruin.
summaries.cot_proverb: He who shows no mercy shall receive none.

fable_text: There was a Master-Pike, who for his size, beauty, and strength, was seen as the Prince of the River. But being the ruler of the freshwater wasn't enough for him; he wanted to rule the sea too. With this ambitious plan, he ventured into the ocean and claimed it as his own. However, a mighty Dolphin took offense at this intrusion and chased the Pike back to the edge of his own stream, barely allowing him to escape. From then on, the Pike wisely stayed within his own territory.
ground_truth_moral: Be content with your lot.

summaries.direct_moral: Know your limits and remain within the sphere where you are best suited.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Overreaching ambition beyond one's natural sphere of influence leads to inevitable humiliation and defeat.
summaries.proverb: Better to be a big fish in a small pond than a small fish in a big pond.
summaries.cot_proverb: Better to be a big fish in a small pond than a small fish in a big ocean.

fable_text: A Sheep that was about to be sheared was upset with the Shepherd for not being content with the Milk she provided, but also taking her Wool. The Shepherd, without further words, angrily took one of the Lambs and killed it. 'Well,' said the Sheep, 'I hope you've done your worst now.' 'No,' said the Shepherd, 'when that's done, I can cut your throat too if I want, and throw you to the Dogs or Wolves as I please.' The Sheep said nothing more, fearing something worse might happen.
ground_truth_moral: When people won't listen to reason through kind means, they must be made to understand through harsh ones.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not provoke those who hold absolute power over your life.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Those at the mercy of a tyrant risk total destruction by protesting lesser grievances.
summaries.proverb: Endure a minor wrong to avoid a greater evil.
summaries.cot_proverb: Better to suffer a small loss than to provoke a greater calamity.

fable_text: The coachman, hearing one of the wheels of his coach creak, was surprised; but more especially when he perceived that it was the worst wheel of the whole set, and which he thought had but little pretense to take such a liberty.
ground_truth_moral: Complaining is the privilege of the weak.

summaries.direct_moral: Those who contribute the least often complain the loudest.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Those who contribute the least are often the loudest in their complaints.
summaries.proverb: The worst wheel creaks the most.
summaries.cot_proverb: The worst wheel of the cart makes the most noise.

fable_text: There was a generous Rich Man who always had a grand and open table, so he never lacked guests. This man noticed that all sorts of people came to him, and he became curious to see who were true friends and who were just freeloaders. So one day, at a full table, he mentioned a quarrel he had and said he was about to seek satisfaction. He needed a certain number of people to join him, and he was sure they would stand by him with swords in hand. Everyone made excuses except for two, whom he counted as his friends, while the rest were just hangers-on.
ground_truth_moral: True friends are hard to come by.

summaries.direct_moral: Adversity is the true test of friendship.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Prosperity gathers many companions, but adversity reveals true friends.
summaries.proverb: Prosperity gains friends, adversity tries them.
summaries.cot_proverb: Prosperity makes friends, but adversity tries them.

fable_text: As a Dog was chasing closely behind a Fox, a Hare suddenly appeared. 'Hold on a moment,' said the Fox, 'and catch that Hare while you can. You've never tasted such a treat in your life. But I'm all spoiled and rotten, and a bite of my flesh would poison you.' The Dog immediately left the Fox and chased after the Hare, but she was too quick for him. When he realized he couldn't catch her, he wisely let her go. The Hare had overheard what happened, and when she met the Fox a few days later, she told him how badly he had treated her. 'Well,' said the Fox, 'if you are upset that I spoke well of you, what would you have done if I had spoken badly?'
ground_truth_moral: A false friend is the worst kind of enemy.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed often leads to losing a certain prize in pursuit of an uncertain one.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Praise from a self-serving source can be more dangerous than open hostility.
summaries.proverb: Self-preservation is the first law of nature.
summaries.cot_proverb: Beware the praise of an enemy.

fable_text: The rabbit and the fox made their petitions to Jupiter: the fox asked that swiftness of foot be added to her slyness, and the rabbit asked that slyness be added to his swiftness. Jupiter responded to them as follows: 'From the beginning of the world, from my breast I have poured forth gifts to each and every animal very generously; if I were to give one animal all the fits, that would be an injury to the others.'
ground_truth_moral: Be content with your lot.

summaries.direct_moral: Nature distributes unique gifts among all to maintain balance and prevent the unfair dominance of any one individual.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Universal balance is maintained by the distribution of diverse talents among all beings to prevent the absolute dominance of any single individual.
summaries.proverb: None is granted every gift.
summaries.cot_proverb: No one is granted every gift.

fable_text: Quite a few horses had been brought to the race track, all adorned with elaborate accoutrements, all except for one, so the other horses made fun of him for being so shabby and unsuited to this type of competition, and they concluded that he would not ever be a winner. But when the time for the running of the race arrived and the trumpet blew the signal to start, all the horses leaped from their starting cages, and only then did it become clear how much the horse who had just been made fun of actually outdistanced the rest of the horses with his speed. When all the others had been left far behind, that horse won the palm of victory.
ground_truth_moral: Appearances can be deceiving.

summaries.direct_moral: True merit is measured by performance, not appearance.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True merit is revealed through performance rather than outward appearance.
summaries.proverb: Don't judge a book by its cover.
summaries.cot_proverb: Appearances are deceptive.

fable_text: A Countryman who was caught up in a lawsuit had a close friend and relative who was a lawyer. He went to him again and again for advice, but the lawyer was always too busy and told him to come back another time. The poor man took a nice, fat kid goat with him on his next visit, and when the lawyer's clerk heard the goat at the door, he let the man in and took him to his master. There, the man explained his case, got the lawyer's opinion, thanked the lawyer for seeing him, and thanked the goat for getting him in, and then went on his way.
ground_truth_moral: Money opens all doors.

summaries.direct_moral: Gifts open doors that friendship alone cannot.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Tangible rewards often secure the attention and favor that kinship and duty cannot.
summaries.proverb: Gifts open every door.
summaries.cot_proverb: A golden key opens every door.

fable_text: A certain young man had married a woman who was also quite young, very beautiful and lusty. Wanting to satisfy her boundless appetite, his loins had gotten so worn out that after just a few days he had gotten thin and looked more dead than alive: he could not walk, he could not stand up, he could do any kind of work, and so like some kind of old man he took his pleasure in the sun. So when he was enjoying some sunny place, warming himself by the sun's heat, it happened that some hunters, who had been chasing a wolf, came that way. When the young man asked them why they hadn't caught the wolf, they replied, 'We were not able to pursue him because of his incredible swiftness.' 'Well,' he said, 'if my wife were in charge of him, she'd put an end to his running.'
ground_truth_moral: Even the strongest can be brought low by unchecked desires.

summaries.direct_moral: Unbridled lust leads to physical ruin.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Excessive indulgence in any passion leads to the depletion of one's strength and vitality.
summaries.proverb: Intemperance consumes vitality.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moderation in all things.

fable_text: An Old Man caught a Boy stealing apples from his orchard. 'Hey,' he said, 'come down from that tree and stop taking my apples.' The Boy ignored him and kept picking. 'Well,' said the Old Man, 'they say herbs have magic just like words do,' and he threw a handful of grass at the Boy. This was so silly that the Young Thief thought the Old Man was joking. But finally, the Old Man said, 'If words and herbs won't work, I'll try stones, because they say stones have power too,' and with that, he got the job done.
ground_truth_moral: Those who won't learn from advice must be taught by experience.

summaries.direct_moral: When gentle persuasion fails to correct a wrongdoer, firmer measures must be employed.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: If gentle means fail to correct a wrongdoer, more forceful measures must be employed.
summaries.proverb: When persuasion fails, force must follow.
summaries.cot_proverb: If words will not do, blows must.

fable_text: As a Nightingale was singing in a bush, down swoops a sneaky Sparrow-Hawk and snatches her from the branch. The poor Nightingale pleaded for her life, saying that her small body wasn't worth it and that there were bigger birds to catch. The Hawk replied, 'Do you think I'm so foolish as to give up a little bird I have for a bigger one I don't have?' Then she offered, 'I'll sing you a lovely song for my life.' 'No, no,' said the Hawk, 'I need food for my belly, not music for my ears.'
ground_truth_moral: A bird in the hand is worth two in the bush.

summaries.direct_moral: Never sacrifice a certain gain for an uncertain, larger prospect.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: A certain small gain is more valuable than a greater one that is only a possibility.
summaries.proverb: A bird in the hand is worth two in the bush.
summaries.cot_proverb: A bird in the hand is worth two in the bush.

fable_text: A Lion, who found it very bothersome to live alone, let the Beasts of the Forest know that he had decided to choose one of them as a Friend and Companion. There was a great commotion about who would be the Favorite and the envy of all the others. The Lion chose a Hog, saying, 'He is true and faithful to his friend and will stand by him in all times and dangers.'
ground_truth_moral: Seek the friendship of those who do not withdraw from offering help even in a time of adversity.

summaries.direct_moral: Loyalty is the most valuable quality in a friend.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Loyalty and steadfastness are the true measures of a friend's worth.
summaries.proverb: Faithfulness is the truest bond of friendship.
summaries.cot_proverb: Fidelity is the truest test of a friend.

fable_text: A Gnat, nearly frozen and hungry, went to a Bee-Hive on a cold morning to ask for help. The Gnat offered to teach music to the Bee's family in exchange for food and shelter. The Bee politely declined, saying, 'I teach all my children my own trade so they can earn a living through hard work.'
ground_truth_moral: Educate your children in those arts which are capable of preserving them from want.

summaries.direct_moral: Practical industry provides the surest security against hardship.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Practical industry and self-sufficiency are more valuable than ornamental skills in ensuring survival and security.
summaries.proverb: A useful trade is the best security.
summaries.cot_proverb: Industry is the best security against want.

fable_text: When a war broke out between the Birds and the Beasts, the Lion called all his subjects from ages sixteen to sixty to gather with weapons at a specific time and place, or face his great displeasure. Among those who showed up were many Asses and Hares. Some of the leaders wanted to dismiss them, thinking they were not fit for service. 'Do not be mistaken,' said the Lion, 'the Asses will do well as trumpeters, and the Hares will make excellent letter-carriers.'
ground_truth_moral: No one is so contemptible that he cannot be of use to us in some way.

summaries.direct_moral: Everyone has a unique utility when placed in the right role.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Every individual possesses unique strengths that can be valuable when applied to the appropriate task.
summaries.proverb: Everyone has their use.
summaries.cot_proverb: Every man has his own use.

fable_text: Once there was a fierce civil war among the Hawks. The gentle and innocent Pigeons, out of pure kindness, sent their representatives to try and make peace between them. While the Hawks were busy fighting each other, they paid no attention to anything else. But as soon as they made peace, they returned to their old habit of attacking the Pigeons. This made the Pigeons realize their mistake and understand the danger of helping a common enemy, which led to their own downfall.
ground_truth_moral: The conflicts of the wicked are the protection of the good.

summaries.direct_moral: Intervening in the quarrels of the wicked often leads to your own destruction.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: It is unwise to help an enemy resolve internal conflicts that serve as your own protection.
summaries.proverb: Peace among the wicked is a scourge to the innocent.
summaries.cot_proverb: He who reconciles his enemies paves the way for his own destruction.

fable_text: There is a story that Death visited an Old Man and told him to come along. The Man tried to excuse himself, saying that the journey to the Other World was too long to take on such short notice, and he asked for a little more time to make his Will before he died. Death replied, 'You have had enough warning, one would think, to have been ready by now.' 'In truth,' said the Old Man, 'this is the first time I have ever seen you in my whole life.' 'That's not true,' said Death, 'for you have seen daily examples of mortality before your eyes, in people of all kinds, ages, and ranks. Isn't the frequent sight of others' deaths a reminder enough to make you think of your own? Your dim and hollow eyes, the loss of your hearing, and the faltering of your other senses should remind you, without much ado, that Death has already taken hold of you. And do you think this is the time to keep putting it off? Your final hour, I tell you, has now come, and there's no thought of a reprieve in the case of Fate.'
ground_truth_moral: We should live as if we always saw Death standing by.

summaries.direct_moral: Always be prepared for the inevitable, as the signs of mortality and aging are constant warnings that death is near.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: One should live in constant readiness for the inevitable, as the signs of mortality are ever-present in both the world and the aging self.
summaries.proverb: Death gives many warnings, but no reprieves.
summaries.cot_proverb: Death waits for no man.

fable_text: There was a certain miserly man who was dying and about to part with his enormous heap of money, ill-gotten as it was. He ordered that the bag of coins be brought to him as he was dying and then asked the bag to whom it would bring pleasure. The bag answered him: 'I'm going to bring pleasure to your heirs, who will squander these coins, over which you sweated, on women and drinks, and I'll also bring pleasure to the demons who are going to turn your soul over to eternal torments.'
ground_truth_moral: A life of greed and hoarding wealth leads to misery.

summaries.direct_moral: Ill-gotten wealth brings only ruin to the soul and provides fuel for the vices of others.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Wealth acquired through greed and injustice serves only to corrupt the successor and condemn the accumulator.
summaries.proverb: Ill-gotten gains bring ruin to the soul and waste to the heir.
summaries.cot_proverb: Ill-gotten wealth is the spendthrift’s joy and the sinner’s ruin.

fable_text: An elm tree sprang up on the bank of a river, and the elm tree mocked the willow tree who was her neighbor for being weak and feeble, as it bent with every surge of the water, no matter how light, while the elm tree praised her own stoutness and strength with extravagant words, because for many years the elm tree had remained unshaken by even the most persistent movement of the stream. On one occasion, however, the elm tree snapped from the tremendous violence of the waves and as she was being dragged along by the waters the willow tree laughed and said: Why are you abandoning me, neighbor? Where is your stoutness now?'
ground_truth_moral: Those who adapt to the times will emerge unscathed.

summaries.direct_moral: It is better to bend and survive than to stand rigid and break.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Flexibility and adaptability often prove more resilient than rigid strength in the face of adversity.
summaries.proverb: Better to bend than to break.
summaries.cot_proverb: Better to bend than to break.
